examples.context: In the studio, the subject named olive is currently focused. Whenever something is wise, it is also principled, so any wise olive would be principled. Being principled guarantees that the olive becomes relaxed. If olive were brilliant, then olive would not be loyal. If olive were strategic, then olive would not be trustworthy. Either olive is brilliant or olive is wise; at least one of those holds. Either olive is not focused or olive is loyal, giving a second alternative. Finally, either olive is not relaxed or olive is trustworthy. These sentences capture every fact, rule, and disjunction that will be used in the reasoning.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that olive is strategic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to examine the collection of facts and conditional statements to see whether olive can be classified as strategic. The goal is to derive a truth value for the proposition strategic(olive).

This premise links wisdom to principled behavior, giving us a way to move from wise to principled later in the proof. It will become useful once we establish that olive is wise.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any wise olive must also be principled.</text>
<logic>principled(olive) :- wise(olive)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a chain that turns principled status into a relaxed condition. When we later get principled, this rule will let us infer relaxed.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any principled olive must be relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(olive) :- principled(olive)</logic>
</step>

This rule tells us that brilliance forces a lack of loyalty. It creates a negative connection that will help us eliminate the possibility of olive being brilliant later on.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if olive is brilliant then olive is not loyal.</text>
<logic>~loyal(olive) :- brilliant(olive)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule provides a bridge from strategic to untrustworthiness. If we ever manage to prove olive is strategic, this rule would give us a contradiction with trustworthiness, which we will later obtain.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if olive is strategic then olive is not trustworthy.</text>
<logic>~trustworthy(olive) :- strategic(olive)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that at least one of brilliant or wise holds for olive. It sets up a binary choice that we will resolve by eliminating the brilliant option.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>One of the two possibilities holds: olive is brilliant or olive is wise.</text>
<logic>brilliant(olive) v wise(olive)</logic>
</step>

This alternative says either olive is not focused or olive is loyal. Combined with the known fact that olive is focused, it forces the loyalty side of the disjunction.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either olive is not focused or olive is loyal.</text>
<logic>~focused(olive) v loyal(olive)</logic>
</step>

The last disjunction provides a link between being not relaxed and being trustworthy. Once we know olive is relaxed, we will be able to pick the trustworthy side.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either olive is not relaxed or olive is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>~relaxed(olive) v trustworthy(olive)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact tells us that olive is focused, giving us a concrete starting point for the disjunctive reasoning that follows.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>It is given as a fact that olive is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(olive)</logic>
</step>

Because we know olive is focused, the part "olive is not focused" in the disjunction cannot be true, so the only way for the statement "not focused or loyal" to hold is for olive to be loyal. This deduces loyalty from the combination of a fact and a disjunction.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">
<text>From the fact that olive is focused and the disjunction that olive is not focused or loyal, we infer that olive must be loyal.</text>
<logic>loyal(olive)</logic>
</step>

The rule "brilliant implies not loyal" tells us that if olive were brilliant, olive would not be loyal. Since we have just derived that olive is loyal, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that olive cannot be brilliant. Thus we obtain the negation of brilliant.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Using the rule that brilliant implies not loyal together with olive being loyal, we conclude that olive cannot be brilliant.</text>
<logic>~brilliant(olive)</logic>
</step>

The earlier disjunction "brilliant or wise" now has its brilliant side ruled out, leaving wise as the only surviving alternative. Therefore, olive must be wise.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Given that olive is not brilliant and that either olive is brilliant or wise, we deduce that olive is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(olive)</logic>
</step>

Having established that olive is wise, we can invoke the first rule (wise → principled) to conclude that olive is principled. This moves us further along the chain toward relaxed.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>Since olive is wise and the rule that wise implies principled, we derive that olive is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(olive)</logic>
</step>

With olive now known to be principled, the second rule (principled → relaxed) lets us infer that olive is relaxed. This prepares us to use the third disjunction concerning relaxed and trustworthy.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>From olive being principled and the rule that principled implies relaxed, we obtain that olive is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(olive)</logic>
</step>

Since olive is relaxed, the "not relaxed" part of the disjunction "not relaxed or trustworthy" is false, forcing the trustworthy side to be true. Consequently, olive is trustworthy.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,7">
<text>Using the fact that olive is relaxed together with the disjunction not relaxed or trustworthy, we infer that olive is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(olive)</logic>
</step>

We now have olive being trustworthy, but the fourth rule tells us that if olive were strategic, olive would be not trustworthy. The only way to keep consistency is to assert that olive is not strategic, giving us the negation of the query.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">
<text>Because olive is trustworthy and the rule that strategic implies not trustworthy, we conclude that olive cannot be strategic.</text>
<logic>~strategic(olive)</logic>
</step>

We derived Olive is not strategic, so the conclusion Olive is strategic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

We have the loom being durable, but the second rule says a high tensile loom would be not durable. Since the loom is durable, the condition for high tensile leading to non‑durability cannot hold, so the loom cannot be high tensile. This is a modus tollens inference.

The fact that the kit is courteous gives us a concrete starting point that activates PREMISE_1. From this we can immediately infer a stocked status.

<logic>~efficient(taylor) v loyal(taylor)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction offers another pair of alternatives: either Taylor is not loyal or Taylor is inventive. After we establish loyalty, the only consistent option is that Taylor is inventive.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Taylor is not loyal or Taylor is inventive.</text>
<logic>~loyal(taylor) v inventive(taylor)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Taylor is efficient is a starting point for using the first disjunction. It directly contradicts the "not efficient" side, forcing us to accept the complementary side, loyalty.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Taylor is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(taylor)</logic>
</step>

Because Taylor is efficient, the clause "not efficient" in the first disjunction is false. To keep the disjunction true, the other disjunct must be true, so we conclude that Taylor is loyal.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>From Taylor being efficient and the first disjunction, we infer that Taylor must be loyal.</text>
<logic>loyal(taylor)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have loyalty, the "not loyal" part of the second disjunction is false. Therefore the remaining disjunct, inventiveness, must hold, giving us that Taylor is inventive.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Using Taylor's loyalty and the second disjunction, we deduce that Taylor is inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(taylor)</logic>
</step>

We have derived that Taylor is inventive. The rule "if consistent then not inventive" tells us that being consistent would make Taylor not inventive, which contradicts what we just found. Hence Taylor cannot be consistent, so we infer ~consistent(taylor).
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Since Taylor is inventive and the rule linking consistency to non‑inventiveness, we conclude that Taylor is not consistent.</text>
<logic>~consistent(taylor)</logic>
</step>

Having established ~consistent(taylor), we can apply the first rule, which states that lack of consistency leads to studiousness. This yields that Taylor is studious.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Given that Taylor is not consistent, the first rule gives us that Taylor is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(taylor)</logic>
</step>

With studiousness in hand, we turn to the third rule: if Taylor were capable, then Taylor would not be studious. Since Taylor is indeed studious, the antecedent (capable) must be false, so we infer ~capable(taylor).
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Because Taylor is studious and the rule that capable implies not studious, we infer that Taylor cannot be capable.</text>
<logic>~capable(taylor)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Taylor is not capable, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the ancient academy, Henry, Iris, and Rose are known for their sharp minds: each of them is analytical, each of them is attentive, and each of them is brave. They also share a steady temperament, being patient individuals. Jamie, another scholar, has a reputation for bravery. The teachers have established several logical guidelines for Jamie: whenever Jamie is perceptive, Jamie is also analytical; whenever Jamie is brave, Jamie is attentive; whenever Jamie is analytical, Jamie is clear_headed; whenever Jamie is resilient, Jamie is optimistic; whenever Jamie is clear_headed, Jamie is patient; whenever Jamie is attentive, Jamie is perceptive; and whenever Jamie is patient, Jamie is resilient.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Jamie is not optimistic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Jamie can be shown to lack optimism. The goal is to see if the knowledge base forces Jamie to be optimistic, which would make the negation false. To do this we will follow the chain of conditional rules that connect Jamie's bravery to optimism. Each rule will be applied step by step, building a forward chain of properties.

Premise 1 gives us the conditional link between perceptiveness and analyticity for Jamie. This is essential because later we will obtain perceptiveness and need to turn it into analyticity. Recognizing this bridge helps us see how a property can cascade forward.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Jamie is perceptive, then Jamie is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(jamie) :- perceptive(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Premise 2 tells us that bravery guarantees attentiveness for Jamie. Since we already have a fact that Jamie is brave, this rule will allow us to derive a new property that starts the chain toward optimism.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Jamie is brave, then Jamie is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(jamie) :- brave(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Premise 3 connects analyticity to clear headedness. Once we manage to prove Jamie is analytical, we will be able to move further along the chain toward patience and eventually resilience.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Jamie is analytical, then Jamie is clear headed.</text>
<logic>clear_headed(jamie) :- analytical(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Premise 4 is the final link in the chain: if Jamie is resilient, then Jamie is optimistic. This rule will be used at the very end of our reasoning to obtain optimism.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Jamie is resilient, then Jamie is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(jamie) :- resilient(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Premise 5 states that clear headedness implies patience. This intermediate step is needed after we have established clear headedness, allowing us to continue toward resilience.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Jamie is clear headed, then Jamie is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(jamie) :- clear_headed(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Premise 6 says that attentiveness leads to perceptiveness. It will let us turn the attentiveness derived from bravery into perceptiveness, which then triggers analyticity.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Jamie is attentive, then Jamie is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(jamie) :- attentive(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Premise 7 provides the link from patience to resilience. After we acquire patience, this rule will let us reach resilience, setting up the final optimism step.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If Jamie is patient, then Jamie is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(jamie) :- patient(jamie)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Jamie is brave is our starting point. It directly activates Premise 2, giving us attentiveness, and therefore initiates the whole forward reasoning process.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Jamie is brave.</text>
<logic>brave(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Jamie is brave together with Premise 2, we can conclude that Jamie is attentive. This is the first derived property after the initial fact, and it will serve as input for the next rule.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>From Jamie being brave and the rule that bravery implies attentiveness, we infer that Jamie is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Jamie attentive, Premise 6 allows us to infer that Jamie is perceptive. This new property is crucial because Premise 1 requires perceptiveness to produce analyticity.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>From Jamie being attentive and the rule that attentiveness implies perceptiveness, we infer that Jamie is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(jamie)</logic>
</step>

With Jamie perceptive established, Premise 1 lets us derive analyticity for Jamie. Analyticity is a pivotal property because it unlocks the rule that leads to clear headedness.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>From Jamie being perceptive and the rule that perceptiveness implies analyticity, we infer that Jamie is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Having obtained analyticity, Premise 3 yields that Jamie is clear headed. Clear headedness is the gateway to patience, moving us further along the chain.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>From Jamie being analytical and the rule that analyticity implies clear headedness, we infer that Jamie is clear headed.</text>
<logic>clear_headed(jamie)</logic>
</step>

From clear headedness, Premise 5 gives us patience for Jamie. Patience is the immediate predecessor of resilience in our conditional network.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,5">
<text>From Jamie being clear headed and the rule that clear headedness implies patience, we infer that Jamie is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Applying Premise 7 to Jamie's patience, we infer that Jamie is resilient. Resilience is the last antecedent before we can claim optimism.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,7">
<text>From Jamie being patient and the rule that patience implies resilience, we infer that Jamie is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Finally, Premise 4 tells us that if Jamie is resilient then Jamie is optimistic. Since we have just derived resilience, we can now assert that Jamie is optimistic.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">
<text>From Jamie being resilient and the rule that resilience implies optimism, we infer that Jamie is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(jamie)</logic>
</step>

We derived Jamie is optimistic, so the conclusion Jamie is not optimistic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Paul is capable. Whenever Paul is grateful, he is also adaptable. Whenever Paul is thorough, he becomes judicious. Whenever Paul is adaptable, he is also thorough. If Paul is sharp, then he is not determined. If Paul is competitive, then he is not methodical. Either Paul is competitive or Paul is grateful. Either Paul is not capable or Paul is methodical. Either Paul is not judicious or Paul is determined.
examples.question: Is it true that Paul is not sharp?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to show that Paul cannot be sharp. The goal is to derive the negation of the predicate sharp for Paul using the supplied facts, rules, and disjunctions. By chaining implications and eliminating alternatives, we will reach the desired conclusion.

This premise tells us that gratitude guarantees adaptability for Paul. It will become useful once we can establish that Paul is grateful.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Paul is grateful, then Paul is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(paul) :- grateful(paul)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that being thorough ensures judiciousness. Later steps will give us thoroughness, allowing us to obtain judiciousness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Paul is thorough, then Paul is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(paul) :- thorough(paul)</logic>
</step>

This rule links adaptability to thoroughness, so once we have adaptability we can move forward to thoroughness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Paul is adaptable, then Paul is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(paul) :- adaptable(paul)</logic>
</step>

The statement says that sharpness forces Paul not to be determined. Later we will use the contrapositive of this rule to rule out sharpness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Paul is sharp, then Paul is not determined.</text>
<logic>~determined(paul) :- sharp(paul)</logic>
</step>

According to this rule, if Paul were competitive, he could not be methodical. This will let us eliminate competitiveness once we know Paul is methodical.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Paul is competitive, then Paul is not methodical.</text>
<logic>~methodical(paul) :- competitive(paul)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a choice between competitiveness and gratitude. When one of these is ruled out, the other must hold.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Paul is competitive or Paul is grateful.</text>
<logic>competitive(paul) v grateful(paul)</logic>
</step>

This disjunction provides a bridge between capability and methodicalness. Since we have capability, we can resolve it to obtain methodicalness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Paul is not capable or Paul is methodical.</text>
<logic>~capable(paul) v methodical(paul)</logic>
</step>

This disjunction connects judiciousness with determination. It will allow us to derive determination once we know Paul is judicious.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either Paul is not judicious or Paul is determined.</text>
<logic>~judicious(paul) v determined(paul)</logic>
</step>

The fact simply states that Paul possesses the capability property, which will be used together with the seventh disjunction.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Paul is capable.</text>
<logic>capable(paul)</logic>
</step>

Because Paul is capable and the seventh premise says either not capable or methodical, the first alternative is false, forcing the second to be true. Hence Paul must be methodical.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,7">
<text>From Paul being capable and the disjunction ‘not capable or methodical’, we conclude that Paul is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(paul)</logic>
</step>

We have a rule that competitive would make Paul not methodical. Since we already have methodical, the only way to avoid a contradiction is that Paul cannot be competitive, giving us not competitive.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Since Paul is methodical and competitive would make him not methodical, we infer that Paul is not competitive.</text>
<logic>~competitive(paul)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise tells us that Paul is either competitive or grateful. Having just shown he is not competitive, the only remaining option is that Paul is grateful.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Because Paul is not competitive and the disjunction ‘competitive or grateful’ holds, we deduce that Paul is grateful.</text>
<logic>grateful(paul)</logic>
</step>

Now that Paul is grateful, the first rule applies: gratitude leads to adaptability. Therefore Paul becomes adaptable.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Given that Paul is grateful and grateful implies adaptable, we obtain that Paul is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(paul)</logic>
</step>

With adaptability established, the third rule kicks in, turning adaptability into thoroughness. So Paul is now thorough.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,3">
<text>From Paul being adaptable and the rule that adaptable leads to thorough, we get that Paul is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(paul)</logic>
</step>

Given that Paul is thorough, the second rule tells us that thoroughness guarantees judiciousness. Thus Paul is judicious.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,2">
<text>Since Paul is thorough and thorough leads to judicious, we derive that Paul is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(paul)</logic>
</step>

The eighth disjunction says either not judicious or determined. Since Paul is judicious, the first part is false, leaving determined as the true statement.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,8">
<text>With Paul being judicious and the disjunction ‘not judicious or determined’, we infer that Paul is determined.</text>
<logic>determined(paul)</logic>
</step>

We know from the fourth rule that if Paul were sharp, he would not be determined. But we have just derived that Paul is determined, so by modus tollens Paul cannot be sharp. Hence not sharp holds.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,4">
<text>Because Paul is determined and sharp would make him not determined, we conclude that Paul is not sharp.</text>
<logic>~sharp(paul)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Paul is not sharp, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the wildlife sanctuary, four animals are known for their kindness: Cora, Lennox, Rose, and Sofia are all generous. Whenever an animal is generous, it also behaves cooperatively; therefore, if Lennox is generous, Lennox is cooperative. Moreover, being adaptable guarantees a lack of cooperation: if Lennox is adaptable, then Lennox is not cooperative. No other rules or exceptions apply.
examples.question: Based on the information provided, can we conclude that Lennox is not adaptable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Lennox lacks the property of being adaptable. To answer this, we will use the given facts and conditional rules that connect generosity, cooperation, and adaptability.

The first conditional tells us that generosity leads to cooperation for Lennox. This is important because it lets us turn a known fact about generosity into a statement about cooperation, which will later be used in a contrapositive argument.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule stating that if Lennox is generous then Lennox is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(lennox) :- generous(lennox)</logic>
</step>

The second conditional links adaptability to a lack of cooperation. By establishing this relationship, we set up a way to reason from the presence of cooperation to the absence of adaptability via contrapositive reasoning.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule stating that if Lennox is adaptable then Lennox is not cooperative.</text>
<logic>~cooperative(lennox) :- adaptable(lennox)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Lennox is generous. This factual premise provides the starting point for applying the first rule and moving forward in the proof.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Lennox is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(lennox)</logic>
</step>

Since Lennox is generous (Premise 3) and the rule says generosity implies cooperation (Premise 1), we can directly infer that Lennox is cooperative. This step follows the standard modus ponens pattern: if P then Q, and P holds, therefore Q holds.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>From Lennox being generous and the first rule, we infer that Lennox is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(lennox)</logic>
</step>

Now we have that Lennox is cooperative (Step 4). The second rule tells us that if Lennox were adaptable, he would not be cooperative. By contraposition (modus tollens), the fact that Lennox is cooperative forces the negation of the antecedent, meaning Lennox cannot be adaptable. Hence we derive ~adaptable(lennox).
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Using the fact that Lennox is cooperative together with the second rule, we infer that Lennox cannot be adaptable.</text>
<logic>~adaptable(lennox)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Lennox is not adaptable, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.question: Is Vera meticulous?

examples.context: The studio houses two notable pieces: a glazed vase and an earthenware mug. The glazed vase is trusted, and the earthenware mug is also trusted. examples.question: Is the earthenware mug abundant?

<logic>sturdy(earthenware_mug) :- glazed(earthenware_mug)</logic>
</step>

Here we are told that trust guarantees glazing for the earthenware mug. Since we already know the mug is trusted, this rule will let us produce a glazing fact that feeds into the previous premise.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the earthenware mug is trusted, then it is glazed.</text>
<logic>glazed(earthenware_mug) :- trusted(earthenware_mug)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a negative relationship: if the mug were abundant, it could not be sturdy. This will become the key to disproving abundance once we establish that the mug is indeed sturdy.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that if the earthenware mug is abundant, then it is not sturdy.</text>
<logic>~sturdy(earthenware_mug) :- abundant(earthenware_mug)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the earthenware mug is trusted is our starting point. It directly activates the second rule, allowing us to move forward in the inference chain.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that the earthenware mug is trusted.</text>
<logic>trusted(earthenware_mug)</logic>
</step>

Using the trusted status of the mug together with the rule that trust implies glazing, we can conclude the mug is glazed. This step translates a known fact into a new property that matches the antecedent of the first rule.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From the fact that the earthenware mug is trusted and the rule linking trust to glazing, we infer that the earthenware mug is glazed.</text>
<logic>glazed(earthenware_mug)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have the mug glazed, the first rule tells us that any glazed mug is also sturdy. Applying this, we derive that the earthenware mug is sturdy, giving us a positive statement about its robustness.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since the earthenware mug is glazed and the rule linking glazing to sturdiness, we conclude that the earthenware mug is sturdy.</text>
<logic>sturdy(earthenware_mug)</logic>

We have established the mug is sturdy, but the third rule says that abundance would force it to be not sturdy. Since the mug is sturdy, the condition for abundance cannot hold, leading us to infer that the mug is not abundant.

Therefore Phenol is is acidic, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: Consider the organic compound aspirin. It is known to be stable. In this chemical framework, any compound that is aromatic is also reactive. Furthermore, regarding aspirin, either it is not stable or it is aromatic.
examples.question: Is aspirin reactive?

The conditional rule is crucial because it provides a direct link from aromaticity to reactivity, which is exactly the property we are trying to establish for aspirin. Recognizing this link lets us later turn an aromatic conclusion into a reactive one.

<logic>reactive(aspirin) :- aromatic(aspirin)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possibilities concerning aspirin: either it lacks stability or it possesses aromatic character. This statement will become useful when we pair it with the known stability fact to eliminate the first alternative.

</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">

</step>

Since we know aspirin is stable, the first option in the disjunction (that aspirin is not stable) cannot hold. By eliminating that possibility, the only remaining way for the disjunction to be true is that aspirin must be aromatic. This application of disjunctive syllogism yields the aromatic conclusion.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Using the fact that aspirin is stable together with the disjunction, we conclude that aspirin is aromatic.</text>
<logic>aromatic(aspirin)</logic>

Now that we have established aspirin is aromatic, we can invoke the earlier rule that every aromatic compound is reactive. Substituting aspirin for the generic compound, we directly infer that aspirin is reactive, achieving the desired property.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Because aspirin is aromatic, the rule lets us infer that aspirin is reactive.</text>
<logic>reactive(aspirin)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Aspirin is reactive, so the conclusion is True.

Answer: True

We are asked to decide whether the given morpheme possesses the property of being productive. The answer will follow from the information about its frequency and the linguistic rules that link frequency to regularity and regularity to productivity.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any morpheme that is frequent is also regular.</text>
<logic>regular(morpheme) :- frequent(morpheme)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that regularity guarantees productivity. It will allow us to move from regularity, once established, to the desired conclusion about productivity.

<text>Any morpheme that is regular is also productive.</text>

We are directly told that the morpheme is frequent, which activates the first rule and starts the chain of inference.

<text>The morpheme is frequent.</text>
<logic>frequent(morpheme)</logic>
</step>

Applying the first rule to the known fact that the morpheme is frequent, we can deduce that it must also be regular. This step follows the logical pattern of modus ponens: a premise and a conditional statement lead to the consequent.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Because the morpheme is frequent and frequent morphemes are regular, we conclude that the morpheme is regular.</text>
<logic>regular(morpheme)</logic>

Now that we have established the morpheme is regular, we can invoke the second rule. Using modus ponens again, the regularity of the morpheme yields its productivity.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since the morpheme is regular and regular morphemes are productive, we infer that the morpheme is productive.</text>

</step>

Therefore Morpheme is productive, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The statement that a heated rock cannot be reinforced sets up a contradiction that will let us infer the rock is not heated once we have reinforcement.

The disjunction tells us that at least one of the following holds: the rock is not dense or it is sedimentary. This will help us move from density to sedimentary status.

The fact that the rock is crystalline is the starting point for applying the first rule and beginning the chain of deductions.

</step>

By applying the rule from PREMISE_1 to the fact from PREMISE_5, we see that the rock must be dense. This gives us a concrete property to work with in the next step.

We now have the rock being dense, and the disjunction from PREMISE_4 says either not dense or sedimentary. Since dense is true, the only way the disjunction can hold is if the rock is sedimentary, so we conclude it is sedimentary.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">

Having established that the rock is sedimentary, we can invoke the rule from PREMISE_2, which tells us that any sedimentary rock is reinforced. Therefore, the rock is reinforced.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">

<logic>reinforced(basalt_rock)</logic>
</step>

We possess two pieces: the rock is reinforced (from STEP_8) and the rule from PREMISE_3 that a heated rock cannot be reinforced. If the rock were heated, it would contradict the reinforcement fact, so we must conclude the rock is not heated.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Because the basalt rock is reinforced and a heated basalt rock cannot be reinforced, we infer that the basalt rock is not heated.</text>
<logic>~heated(basalt_rock)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Basalt_rock is not heated, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: In the game design world, Gwen is known to be consistent, and Mark is known to be honest. Conversely, being scholarly forces a person to be not diplomatic, so if Mark were scholarly he could not be diplomatic. Additionally, either Gwen is not creative or she is insightful, and either Mark is not honest or he is reflective. These are all the facts, rules, and alternatives that shape the situation.
examples.question: Based on the information given, can we conclude that Mark is not scholarly?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Mark lacks the scholarly attribute. The goal is to use the supplied facts, rules, and disjunctions to see if the negation of scholarly for Mark follows logically. We'll start by examining the explicit facts and then apply the conditional statements step by step.

The first rule tells us that insight leads to capability for Gwen. Although this rule does not involve Mark directly, we note it because it may become relevant if we ever derive that Gwen is insightful. Keeping it in mind ensures we have the full set of logical connections available.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Gwen is insightful, then Gwen is capable.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(mark) :- reflective(mark)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links consistency to creativity for Gwen. Since we already know Gwen is consistent, this rule will allow us later to infer her creativity if needed. It establishes a forward chain from a known fact to another property of Gwen.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Gwen is consistent, then Gwen is creative.</text>
<logic>~diplomatic(mark) :- scholarly(mark)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that Mark being reflective guarantees that he is diplomatic. This creates a bridge from the reflective property (which we will soon obtain) to the diplomatic property, which is crucial for the later contradiction with scholarly.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Mark is reflective, then Mark is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>~honest(mark) v reflective(mark)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule provides a contrapositive relationship: if Mark were scholarly, he would not be diplomatic. This will be essential for a modus tollens step once we have diplomatic, allowing us to rule out scholarly.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Mark is scholarly, then Mark is not diplomatic.</text>
<logic>honest(mark)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>By Disjunctive Syllogism, one disjunct is eliminated, leaving Mark is reflective.</text>
<logic>reflective(mark)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Mark is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(mark)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>By Modus Tollens, the consequent is false, so the antecedent must be false: Mark is not scholarly.</text>
<logic>~scholarly(mark)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Mark is not scholarly, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the workshop, the carpenter named Lake is known to be brilliant, and the carpenter named Rio is also brilliant. Whenever a carpenter is brilliant, that carpenter is intuitive. Whenever a carpenter is intuitive, that carpenter is determined. Whenever a carpenter is determined, that carpenter is reliable. Whenever a carpenter is reliable, that carpenter is motivated. If a carpenter is curious, then that carpenter is not motivated. No other facts or rules are given.
examples.question: Is it true that Rio is not curious?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the carpenter Rio can be described as not curious. The goal is to use the given facts about Rio's brilliance and the chain of conditional rules to see if curiosity leads to a contradiction.

The rule linking intuition to determination will be needed later once we establish that Rio is intuitive. It tells us that intuition is sufficient for being determined, which is a crucial step in the chain.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Rio is intuitive, then Rio is determined.</text>
<logic>determined(rio) :- intuitive(rio)</logic>
</step>

This rule connects brilliance to intuition, allowing us to move from the known fact that Rio is brilliant to a new property, intuition. It sets up the forward‑chaining sequence of implications.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Rio is brilliant, then Rio is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(rio) :- brilliant(rio)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a rule that makes motivation follow from reliability. Once we can prove Rio is reliable, this rule will let us infer motivation, which is essential for the final contradiction with curiosity.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Rio is reliable, then Rio is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(rio) :- reliable(rio)</logic>
</step>

Reliability depends on determination according to this rule. After we obtain determination, we will be able to claim Rio is reliable, linking the earlier steps to motivation.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Rio is determined, then Rio is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(rio) :- determined(rio)</logic>
</step>

This rule is the only one that involves curiosity. It says that if Rio were curious, he would necessarily be not motivated. We will use this together with the motivation we later derive to block the possibility of curiosity.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Rio is curious, then Rio is not motivated.</text>
<logic>~motivated(rio) :- curious(rio)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Rio is brilliant is the starting point of our reasoning. It gives us a concrete property that we can feed into the intuition rule.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Rio is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(rio)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule that brilliance implies intuition, we turn the fact that Rio is brilliant into the new fact that Rio is intuitive. This is a direct use of the second premise.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Rio being brilliant we infer that Rio is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(rio)</logic>
</step>

With Rio now known to be intuitive, we invoke the first rule which states that intuition guarantees determination. Thus we can safely assert that Rio is determined.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since Rio is intuitive, we can conclude that Rio is determined.</text>
<logic>determined(rio)</logic>
</step>

Since Rio is determined, the fourth rule tells us that determination leads to reliability. Consequently, Rio must be reliable.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Because Rio is determined, we deduce that Rio is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(rio)</logic>
</step>

Having established Rio's reliability, we use the third rule which makes motivation follow from reliability. Therefore Rio is motivated.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Given that Rio is reliable, we infer that Rio is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(rio)</logic>
</step>

We now have Rio motivated, but the fifth rule says that if Rio were curious, he would be not motivated. This creates a direct conflict: Rio cannot be both motivated and not motivated. To avoid the contradiction, we conclude that Rio cannot be curious, i.e., Rio is not curious.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Because Rio is motivated, and if Rio were curious he would be not motivated, we conclude that Rio is not curious.</text>
<logic>~curious(rio)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Rio is not curious, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Elliot is organized. Whenever something is organized, it is also responsible. If something is patient, then it is fit. If something is humble, then it is not fit. Either Elliot is not responsible or Elliot is patient.
examples.question: Is Elliot humble?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the description 'humble' applies to Elliot. The available information includes a fact about Elliot's organization and several conditional relationships among humility, fitness, responsibility, and patience, which we will chain together to reach a conclusion.

This premise links patience to fitness, so if we ever establish that Elliot is patient, we can immediately obtain his fitness status. It will become useful later when we derive patient from other information.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Elliot is patient, then Elliot is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(elliot) :- patient(elliot)</logic>
</step>

The second premise tells us that organization guarantees responsibility. Since we already know Elliot is organized, this rule will allow us to deduce his responsibility, a key ingredient for the upcoming disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Elliot is organized, then Elliot is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(elliot) :- organized(elliot)</logic>
</step>

The third premise provides a contrapositive relation: humility forces the negation of fitness. This will be essential for the final step where we will use the established fitness of Elliot to rule out humility.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Elliot is humble, then Elliot is not fit.</text>
<logic>~fit(elliot) :- humble(elliot)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: either Elliot lacks responsibility or he is patient. Once we know whether Elliot is responsible, we can eliminate one side and possibly obtain the other, giving us patient.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The knowledge base includes the disjunction that either Elliot is not responsible or Elliot is patient.</text>
<logic>~responsible(elliot) v patient(elliot)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Elliot is organized is our starting concrete piece of information. It will trigger the responsible‑from‑organized rule, setting the chain of inference in motion.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>A fact tells us that Elliot is organized.</text>
<logic>organized(elliot)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens to the organized fact and the rule that organized implies responsible, we can safely assert that Elliot is responsible. This new fact is needed to evaluate the disjunction in the next step.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From Elliot being organized and the rule that organized implies responsible, we infer that Elliot is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(elliot)</logic>
</step>

We now have responsible(Elliot). The disjunction says "not responsible or patient". Since responsible(Elliot) makes the first option false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the second option, patient(Elliot), to be true. Thus we infer that Elliot is patient.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Using the fact that Elliot is responsible together with the disjunction not responsible or patient, we conclude that Elliot is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(elliot)</logic>
</step>

Having derived patient(Elliot), we can use the earlier rule that patient leads to fit. By modus ponens, this gives us fit(Elliot), establishing that Elliot possesses the fitness property.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since Elliot is patient and the rule that patient implies fit, we derive that Elliot is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(elliot)</logic>
</step>

We possess fit(Elliot) and the rule that humble implies not fit. Using modus tollens, the truth of fit(Elliot) forces the negation of the antecedent, so we conclude that Elliot cannot be humble, i.e., ~humble(Elliot).
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Given that Elliot is fit and the rule that humble implies not fit, modus tollens lets us infer that Elliot is not humble.</text>
<logic>~humble(elliot)</logic>
</step>

We derived Elliot is not humble, so the conclusion Elliot is humble is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In a medical training program, Jamie and Quinn each have a set of professional qualities. Jamie is skillful, and Quinn is also skillful. Quinn, however, is not well prepared. The program’s guidelines state several conditional relationships that apply specifically to Jamie: if Jamie is rational then Jamie is industrious; if Jamie is optimistic then Jamie is insightful; if Jamie is industrious then Jamie is optimistic; if Jamie is not well prepared then Jamie is persistent; if Jamie is skillful then Jamie is rational; and if Jamie is well prepared then Jamie is not insightful. Additionally, the guidelines include a disjunctive statement about Jamie: either Jamie is not persistent or Jamie is astute. No other default rules are in effect.
examples.question: Given the information above, can we conclude that Jamie is astute?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Jamie possesses the quality of being astute based on the provided facts and conditional statements. The goal is to chain the available rules so that we can either satisfy or refute the disjunctive condition that involves astuteness.

This premise connects rationality to industriousness for Jamie, which will be useful later when we derive rationality from skillfulness. Recognizing this link lets us later turn a rational fact into an industrious one.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that Jamie being rational guarantees that Jamie is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(jamie) :- rational(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Here we see that optimism leads to insight for Jamie. Once we obtain optimism, this rule will allow us to infer that Jamie is insightful, advancing our chain toward the final goal.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that Jamie being optimistic guarantees that Jamie is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(jamie) :- optimistic(jamie)</logic>
</step>

This rule tells us that Jamie's industriousness yields optimism. It creates a bridge from any derived industriousness to the optimism needed for insight.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that Jamie being industrious guarantees that Jamie is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(jamie) :- industrious(jamie)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise states that a lack of preparation forces persistence. It will become relevant after we establish that Jamie is not well prepared, allowing us to claim persistence.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Jamie is not well prepared then Jamie is persistent.</text>
<logic>rational(jamie) :- skillful(jamie)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise directly converts skillfulness into rationality for Jamie. Since we already know Jamie is skillful, this rule is the starting point for generating rationality.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that Jamie being skillful guarantees that Jamie is rational.</text>
<logic>~insightful(jamie) :- well_prepared(jamie)</logic>
</step>

This premise provides a way to obtain a negative insight condition from well‑preparedness. Although we do not yet know Jamie's preparation status, the rule will later help us deduce that Jamie cannot be well prepared.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Jamie is well prepared then Jamie is not insightful.</text>
<logic>~persistent(jamie) v astute(jamie)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two alternatives: either Jamie is not persistent or Jamie is astute. If we can show that Jamie is persistent, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Jamie must be astute.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction states that either Jamie is not persistent or Jamie is astute.</text>
<logic>skillful(jamie)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>By Universal Instantiation, grounding the quantified rule for this entity gives Jamie is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(jamie)</logic>
</step>

By applying the rule that skillful implies rational to the known fact that Jamie is skillful, we obtain rationality for Jamie. This step is the first logical move that transforms a concrete fact into a new property needed for later steps.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From Jamie being skillful and the rule that skillful implies rational, we infer that Jamie is rational.</text>
<logic>industrious(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have rational(jamie), we can invoke the earlier premise linking rationality to industriousness. Modus ponens lets us conclude that Jamie is industrious, moving us further along the chain toward optimism.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Using Jamie’s rationality together with the rule that rational implies industrious, we conclude Jamie is industrious.</text>
<logic>optimistic(jamie)</logic>
</step>

With Jamie established as industrious, we use the rule that industrious guarantees optimism. This yields optimistic(jamie), which is essential for the upcoming insight inference.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because Jamie is industrious and the rule that industrious implies optimistic, we deduce Jamie is optimistic.</text>
<logic>insightful(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Having derived optimism, we apply the rule that optimism leads to insight. Consequently, Jamie becomes insightful, bringing us to a point where we can examine the insight‑related negative rule.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>With Jamie being optimistic and the rule that optimistic implies insightful, we obtain that Jamie is insightful.</text>
<logic>~well_prepared(jamie)</logic>
</step>

We now have insightful(jamie) together with the rule that well‑preparedness would produce not‑insightful. Since Jamie is insightful, the antecedent "well prepared" would cause a contradiction, so we infer that Jamie cannot be well prepared; thus ~well_prepared(jamie) holds.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12">
<text>Since Jamie is insightful and the rule that well‑prepared leads to not‑insightful, we can infer that Jamie cannot be well prepared; thus Jamie is not well prepared.</text>
<logic>persistent(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Given the newly established ~well_prepared(jamie) and the rule that a lack of preparation forces persistence, we can assert that Jamie is persistent. This fulfills the positive side of the disjunction’s left alternative.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,6">
<text>Given that Jamie is not well prepared and the rule that not‑well‑prepared leads to persistent, we infer Jamie is persistent.</text>
<logic>astute(jamie)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Jamie is astute, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The disjunction says the foundation must be either sustainable or precise. This alternative will become decisive once we rule out one of the options.

We are directly told that the foundation is structural, which is the same as being analytical in the original terminology. This fact will trigger the modus tollens argument.

</step>

Because the foundation is structural, and the rule says a sustainable foundation would be not structural, we can apply modus tollens: the consequent (not structural) is false, so the antecedent (sustainable) must be false. Hence the foundation is not sustainable.

<text>Since the foundation is structural and a sustainable foundation would be not structural, we infer that the foundation is not sustainable.</text>

<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Given that the foundation is not sustainable and that it must be either sustainable or precise, we deduce it must be precise.</text>
<logic>precise(foundation)</logic>

With the foundation established as precise, we invoke the first rule which tells us that precision guarantees aesthetic quality. Therefore, the foundation is aesthetic.

<logic>aesthetic(foundation)</logic>
</step>

Since the foundation is aesthetic, the second rule applies, converting aesthetic into functional. Consequently, the foundation is functional.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Since the foundation is aesthetic and aesthetic implies functional, we infer the foundation is functional.</text>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If David is curious, then David is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(david) :- curious(david)</logic>
</step>

This conditional directly links the absence of calmness to friendliness. Our ultimate goal is friendliness, so once we can establish that David is not calm, this premise will give us the desired conclusion.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If David is not calm, then David is friendly.</text>
<logic>friendly(david) :- ~calm(david)</logic>
</step>

The statement says that calmness forces a lack of confidence. It will become relevant after we have confidence for David; together they will allow us to deduce that David cannot be calm through contraposition.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If David is calm, then David is not confident.</text>
<logic>~confident(david) :- calm(david)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides a logical alternative: either David is not fit or he is diligent. When we later learn that David is fit, the first option becomes false, forcing the second option—diligence—to hold.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either David is not fit or David is diligent.</text>
<logic>~fit(david) v diligent(david)</logic>
</step>

This is the factual basis that David possesses curiosity. It is the starting point for deriving fitness, which then triggers the chain of reasoning toward friendliness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>David is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(david)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that David is curious together with the rule that curiosity implies fitness, we can directly infer that David is fit. This establishes the left side of the disjunction in PREMISE_6, allowing us to later eliminate the ~fit alternative.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>From David being curious and the rule that curiosity makes one fit, we infer that David is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(david)</logic>
</step>

We now have David fit, which makes the first disjunct (~fit) in PREMISE_6 false. Because a disjunction must be true, the only way for it to hold is for the second disjunct, diligent(david), to be true. Thus we conclude David is diligent.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">
<text>Since we have David fit and the disjunction that David is not fit or diligent, we conclude David must be diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(david)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that David is diligent, we can apply PREMISE_1, which says diligent implies brilliant. Therefore, we can conclude that David is brilliant, moving us forward toward confidence.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Given David is diligent and the rule that diligence leads to brilliance, we deduce David is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(david)</logic>
</step>

With David brilliant established, PREMISE_2 tells us that brilliance guarantees confidence. Consequently, we deduce that David is confident, an essential piece for the next contraposition step.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>From David's brilliance and the rule that brilliance yields confidence, we obtain that David is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(david)</logic>
</step>

We now have confidence for David, while PREMISE_5 states that calmness leads to not confidence. Since David is confident, the consequent of PREMISE_5 would be false if David were calm. By modus tollens, we infer that David cannot be calm, i.e., ~calm(david).
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>Because David is confident and the rule that calm implies not confidence, we infer that David cannot be calm.</text>
<logic>~calm(david)</logic>
</step>

Finally, we possess ~calm(david). PREMISE_4 directly says that lack of calmness results in friendliness. Therefore, we can safely infer that David is friendly, achieving the desired conclusion.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>With David not being calm and the rule that non‑calm leads to friendliness, we conclude David is friendly.</text>
<logic>friendly(david)</logic>
</step>

Therefore David is friendly, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In this organic‑chemistry scenario, the molecule acetone is described as stable, and the molecule benzene is also described as stable. The chemical principles we rely on are: whenever a molecule is stable, it is catalytic, and whenever a molecule is reactive, it cannot be catalytic. No other facts, disjunctions, or default rules are provided.
examples.question: Is the benzene molecule reactive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the benzene molecule possesses the reactive property. To answer, we will use the given facts about stability and the two chemical rules linking stability, catalytic ability, and reactivity.

The first rule tells us that stability implies catalytic behavior. This relationship will allow us to move from a fact about benzene's stability to a statement about its catalytic nature.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any molecule that is stable is catalytic.</text>
<logic>catalytic(benzene) :- stable(benzene)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that reactivity forces a molecule to be non‑catalytic. This contrapositive link will be crucial for showing that benzene cannot be reactive once we know it is catalytic.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a molecule is reactive then it is not catalytic.</text>
<logic>~catalytic(benzene) :- reactive(benzene)</logic>
</step>

The fact that benzene is stable gives us a concrete starting point. Knowing its stability lets us apply the first rule directly.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Benzene is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(benzene)</logic>
</step>

Applying the first rule to the stable benzene, we deduce that benzene must be catalytic. This step follows the logical pattern of modus ponens: from "stable implies catalytic" and "benzene is stable" we obtain "benzene is catalytic".
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Because benzene is stable and stability guarantees catalytic behavior, we infer that benzene is catalytic.</text>
<logic>catalytic(benzene)</logic>
</step>

Now we have benzene as catalytic, but the second rule says a reactive molecule would be non‑catalytic. By contraposition (modus tollens), if benzene were reactive, it would have to be non‑catalytic, which contradicts the catalytic status we just derived. Hence benzene cannot be reactive.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since benzene is catalytic, and a reactive molecule would have to be non‑catalytic, we conclude that benzene cannot be reactive.</text>
<logic>~reactive(benzene)</logic>
</step>

We derived Benzene is not reactive, so the conclusion Benzene is reactive is False.
</think>
Answer: False

<logic>diligent(derek) :- agile(derek)</logic>
</step>

The third rule connects the absence of skillfulness to diplomacy. Later, when we show Derek lacks skill, we can immediately label him diplomatic.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Anyone who is not skillful is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>enthusiastic(derek) :- creative(derek)</logic>
</step>

This conditional states that creativity guarantees enthusiasm. Since Derek is known to be creative, this rule will help us start the chain by giving us enthusiasm.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Being creative guarantees that a person is enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>industrious(derek) :- diligent(derek)</logic>
</step>

According to this premise, diligence implies industriousness. After we obtain Derek's diligence, we can use this to get his industriousness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If a person is diligent, then they are industrious.</text>
<logic>~industrious(derek) :- skillful(derek)</logic>
</step>

This rule says that being skillful forces a person to be not industrious. It will be invoked in a contrapositive style: if someone is industrious, they cannot be skillful, which will let us infer a lack of skillfulness for Derek.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If a person is skillful, then they are not industrious.</text>
<logic>~diplomatic(derek) v thorough(derek)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities for Derek: either he is not diplomatic or he is thorough. Our strategy is to show Derek is diplomatic, which will eliminate the first alternative and force the second.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>For Derek, either he is not diplomatic or he is thorough.</text>
<logic>creative(derek)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>By Universal Instantiation, grounding the quantified rule for this entity gives Derek is enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>enthusiastic(derek)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Derek is creative together with the rule that creative individuals are enthusiastic, we conclude Derek is enthusiastic. This establishes the first link needed for the chain toward thoroughness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From Derek being creative we infer that Derek is enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>agile(derek)</logic>
</step>

Now that Derek is enthusiastic, the rule linking enthusiasm to agility lets us infer Derek is agile. This moves us one step closer to establishing his diligence.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Because Derek is enthusiastic, we can conclude that Derek is agile.</text>
<logic>diligent(derek)</logic>
</step>

With Derek's agility in hand, the rule that agile people are diligent gives us Derek's diligence. This prepares us to apply the rule about diligent individuals being industrious.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Since Derek is agile, it follows that Derek is diligent.</text>
<logic>industrious(derek)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule that diligent persons are industrious, we get Derek's industriousness. This fact will be crucial for the next contrapositive inference about skillfulness.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>Given Derek's diligence, we deduce that Derek is industrious.</text>
<logic>~skillful(derek)</logic>
</step>

We have Derek as industrious, and the rule stating that skillful individuals are not industrious tells us that if someone were skillful they could not be industrious. Since Derek is industrious, the only consistent conclusion is that he is not skillful.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12">
<text>From Derek being industrious and the rule that skillful people are not industrious, we infer that Derek is not skillful.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(derek)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Derek is not skillful, the rule that non‑skillful people are diplomatic allows us to assert that Derek is diplomatic. This directly addresses one side of the disjunction in PREMISE_7.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,6">
<text>Because Derek is not skillful, the rule about non‑skillful people lets us conclude that Derek is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>thorough(derek)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Derek is thorough, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Nora is a strong individual. Whenever someone is strong, they are also compassionate, so Nora’s strength makes her compassionate. Any organized person is open‑minded, which means that if Nora were organized she would be open‑minded. Moreover, anyone who is reliable is organized, so a reliable Nora would be organized. Being open‑minded guarantees reflectiveness, so an open‑minded Nora would be reflective. If someone is enthusiastic, then they are reliable, giving a path from enthusiasm to reliability. Finally, either Nora is not compassionate or she is enthusiastic; at least one of those two statements holds.
examples.question: Is Nora reflective?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Nora possesses the reflective quality. The problem supplies a network of conditional statements linking various personal traits, plus a single factual assertion about Nora’s strength, so we must follow the logical chain from the known fact to the target property.

The first rule tells us that strength guarantees compassion. This connection will let us move from the known fact about Nora’s strength toward other traits that depend on compassion.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Nora is strong, then she is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(nora) :- strong(nora)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links organization to open‑mindedness. Although we do not yet know whether Nora is organized, this rule will become useful later when we can establish her organization.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Nora is organized, then she is open‑minded.</text>
<logic>open_minded(nora) :- organized(nora)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that reliability implies organization. This provides a route from reliability to the organized status we need for the previous rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Nora is reliable, then she is organized.</text>
<logic>organized(nora) :- reliable(nora)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that being open‑minded leads to reflectiveness. This is the final step we need to reach the query, so once we have open‑mindedness we can immediately infer reflectiveness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Nora is open‑minded, then she is reflective.</text>
<logic>reflective(nora) :- open_minded(nora)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule connects enthusiasm to reliability. If we can show Nora is enthusiastic, we will be able to chain forward to reliability, then organization, then open‑mindedness, and finally reflectiveness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Nora is enthusiastic, then she is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(nora) :- enthusiastic(nora)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a logical alternative: either Nora is not compassionate or she is enthusiastic. Combined with other information, this will allow us to deduce enthusiasm.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Nora is not compassionate or she is enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>~compassionate(nora) v enthusiastic(nora)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Nora is strong is our starting point. It activates the first rule and begins the cascade of deductions.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Nora is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(nora)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Nora is strong together with the rule that strength implies compassion, we can assert that Nora is compassionate. This is a direct application of modus ponens, moving us from a known premise to a new property.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From Nora’s strength and the rule that strength implies compassion, we infer that Nora is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(nora)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Nora as compassionate, but the disjunction tells us that at least one of ‘not compassionate’ or ‘enthusiastic’ must hold. Since ‘not compassionate’ contradicts the compassionate fact, the only remaining option is that Nora is enthusiastic. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism, eliminating the impossible alternative.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">
<text>Given that Nora is compassionate and the disjunction that she is either not compassionate or enthusiastic, we conclude that she must be enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>enthusiastic(nora)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Nora is enthusiastic, we apply the rule that enthusiasm guarantees reliability. By modus ponens again, we infer that Nora is reliable, advancing us further along the chain toward reflectiveness.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Since Nora is enthusiastic and enthusiasm leads to reliability, we deduce that Nora is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(nora)</logic>
</step>

With Nora’s reliability confirmed, the rule linking reliability to organization lets us conclude that Nora is organized. This step continues the forward reasoning from reliability to the next prerequisite trait.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Because Nora is reliable and reliability leads to being organized, we establish that Nora is organized.</text>
<logic>organized(nora)</logic>
</step>

Since Nora is now known to be organized, the rule stating that organization yields open‑mindedness allows us to deduce that she is open‑minded. This brings us one step closer to the target property.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>With Nora being organized and the rule that organization yields open‑mindedness, we infer that Nora is open‑minded.</text>
<logic>open_minded(nora)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule that open‑mindedness implies reflectiveness lets us infer that Nora is reflective. This directly satisfies the query, completing the logical derivation.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Finally, from Nora’s open‑mindedness and the rule that open‑mindedness implies reflectiveness, we conclude that Nora is reflective.</text>
<logic>reflective(nora)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Nora is reflective, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

<logic>patient(rowan)</logic>
</step>

We combine the fact that Rowan is patient with the disjunction "not patient or humble". Because the patient part makes the "not patient" side false, the disjunction can only be satisfied by Rowan being humble. Thus we deduce humility.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>From Rowan being patient and the disjunction that either Rowan is not patient or humble, we infer that Rowan must be humble.</text>
<logic>humble(rowan)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have humility, we apply Premise 2, which states humility implies principledness. By modus ponens, humility yields that Rowan is principled, giving us a new attribute to work with.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Given Rowan is humble and the rule that humble implies principled, we conclude that Rowan is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(rowan)</logic>
</step>

With principledness established, we look at Premise 6: "not principled or capable". Since the "not principled" option is false, the disjunction forces the other alternative, making Rowan capable.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>Because Rowan is principled and the disjunction that either not principled or capable holds, we deduce that Rowan is capable.</text>
<logic>capable(rowan)</logic>
</step>

Having obtained capability, we use Premise 1, which converts capability into athleticism. This forward chain gives us that Rowan is athletic, an important fact for the next contrapositive step.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>Since Rowan is capable, the rule that capability leads to athleticity lets us assert that Rowan is athletic.</text>
<logic>athletic(rowan)</logic>
</step>

We now have athleticism and Premise 3, which says that trustworthiness would lead to non‑athleticness. Because Rowan is athletic, the consequent of that conditional cannot hold, so the antecedent (trustworthiness) must be false. Hence we infer that Rowan is not trustworthy.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>With Rowan athletic and the rule that a trustworthy person would be not athletic, we infer that Rowan cannot be trustworthy.</text>
<logic>~trustworthy(rowan)</logic>
</step>

Finally, we refer back to Premise 4, the disjunction "trustworthy or skillful". Since we have just shown Rowan is not trustworthy, the only way for the disjunction to remain true is for Rowan to be skillful. Therefore, we conclude skillfulness.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Because Rowan is not trustworthy and the disjunction that either trustworthy or skillful must be true, we conclude that Rowan is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(rowan)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Rowan is skillful, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Helen is clever. Whenever someone is clever, they are strong, so Helen being clever guarantees that she is strong. If a person is strong, then they are talented; therefore Helen's strength makes her talented. Any talented individual is also fair‑minded, which means Helen would be fair‑minded if she were talented. If Helen were prepared, she would not be fair‑minded. Finally, either Helen is prepared or she is evidence‑driven.
examples.question: Is it true that Helen is not evidence‑driven?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the claim that Helen is not evidence‑driven holds. The goal is to see if the premises force Helen to be evidence‑driven, which would make the negation false.

This rule tells us that talent leads to fairness in mind. It will become useful once we can establish Helen's talent, linking it to her fairness status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is talented then she is fair‑minded.</text>
<logic>fair_minded(helen) :- talented(helen)</logic>
</step>

The rule connects cleverness with strength. Since we already know Helen is clever, this rule is the first bridge toward deriving further properties.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is clever then she is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(helen) :- clever(helen)</logic>
</step>

Here strength is linked to talent. Once we have Helen's strength, we can move forward to claim she is talented.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is strong then she is talented.</text>
<logic>talented(helen) :- strong(helen)</logic>
</step>

This conditional states that preparation would contradict fairness. It will later help us eliminate the possibility that Helen is prepared.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is prepared then she is not fair‑minded.</text>
<logic>~fair_minded(helen) :- prepared(helen)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two exclusive possibilities for Helen: either she is prepared or she is evidence‑driven. It creates a logical choice that will be resolved later.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Helen is either prepared or evidence‑driven.</text>
<logic>prepared(helen) v evidence_driven(helen)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Helen is clever is the starting point of the chain. It directly activates the clever‑to‑strong rule.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Helen is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(helen)</logic>
</step>

Because Helen is clever and the rule "clever implies strong" holds, we can confidently assert that Helen is strong. This step converts the known fact into a new property that will feed the next rule.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Helen being clever and the rule that clever implies strong, we infer that Helen is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(helen)</logic>
</step>

With Helen now known to be strong, the rule "strong implies talented" lets us derive that Helen is talented. This derivation is essential for later applying the talent‑to‑fair‑minded rule.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Using Helen's newly derived strength and the rule that strong implies talented, we conclude that Helen is talented.</text>
<logic>talented(helen)</logic>
</step>

Having established Helen's talent, the rule that talent leads to fair‑mindedness gives us Helen's fairness. This result will be crucial when we consider the prepared‑to‑not‑fair‑minded rule.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Because Helen is talented and the rule that talented implies fair‑minded, we obtain that Helen is fair‑minded.</text>
<logic>fair_minded(helen)</logic>
</step>

We now have Helen being fair‑minded, but the rule says that if she were prepared she would not be fair‑minded. Since she is fair‑minded, the antecedent (prepared) must be false; thus Helen cannot be prepared. This negation sets up the final resolution of the disjunction.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Since Helen is fair‑minded and the rule that prepared leads to not fair‑minded, we deduce that Helen cannot be prepared.</text>
<logic>~prepared(helen)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states Helen is either prepared or evidence‑driven. Because we have just shown she is not prepared, the only remaining way for the disjunction to hold is that Helen is evidence‑driven. Hence we infer her evidence‑driven status.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Given that Helen is not prepared and that either she is prepared or evidence‑driven, we infer that she must be evidence‑driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(helen)</logic>
</step>

We derived Helen is evidence driven, so the conclusion Helen is not evidence driven is False.
</think>
Answer: False

Since we have shown Helen is strong, the initial rule that strength implies anxiety lets us finally deduce that Helen is anxious.
