examples.context: Oscar is insightful. Whenever someone is knowledgeable, they are cooperative, so Oscar would be cooperative if he were knowledgeable. Whenever someone is insightful, they are methodical, which means Oscar becomes methodical because he is insightful. If a person is not decisive, then that person is patient; thus a lack of decisiveness guarantees patience. If a person is decisive, then that person is not cooperative, establishing a link from decisiveness to non‑cooperativeness. Finally, either Oscar is not methodical or Oscar is knowledgeable; at least one of these two alternatives must hold.
examples.question: Is it true that Oscar is not patient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Oscar fails to be patient. To answer this, we must use the supplied facts, rules, and the disjunctive information to see if we can derive Oscar's patience status.

This premise connects knowledge to cooperativeness, which will become useful later when we establish that Oscar is knowledgeable. Knowing that knowledge forces cooperation lets us later link Oscar's knowledge to a cooperative status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any knowledgeable individual is also cooperative, so Oscar would be cooperative if he were knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>cooperative(oscar) :- knowledgeable(oscar)</logic>
</step>

This premise links insight to methodical behavior. Since we already know Oscar is insightful, this rule will allow us to infer that he is methodical, a step needed for the disjunctive reasoning that follows.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any insightful individual is also methodical, meaning Oscar becomes methodical because he is insightful.</text>
<logic>methodical(oscar) :- insightful(oscar)</logic>
</step>

This premise tells us how the absence of decisiveness guarantees patience. If we can later show Oscar is not decisive, we will immediately obtain his patience from this rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule declares that if someone is not decisive, then that someone is patient; thus a lack of decisiveness guarantees patience for Oscar.</text>
<logic>patient(oscar) :- ~decisive(oscar)</logic>
</step>

Here we have the contrapositive relationship: decisiveness would prevent cooperation. This will help us later to infer that Oscar cannot be decisive once we have shown he is cooperative.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule tells us that if someone is decisive, then that someone is not cooperative, linking decisiveness to non‑cooperativeness for Oscar.</text>
<logic>~cooperative(oscar) :- decisive(oscar)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possible ways to satisfy the knowledge‑methodical link: either Oscar is not methodical or he is knowledgeable. Once we have established Oscar’s methodical status, this clause will force the other alternative, giving us knowledge.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either Oscar is not methodical or Oscar is knowledgeable; at least one of these alternatives must be true.</text>
<logic>~methodical(oscar) v knowledgeable(oscar)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Oscar is insightful is the starting point for many of the derivations. It directly activates the rule that turns insight into methodicalness.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>We are given the fact that Oscar is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(oscar)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Oscar is insightful together with the rule that insight implies methodicalness, we can conclude Oscar is methodical. This gives us the positive side of the disjunction needed for the next step.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Oscar being insightful and the rule that insight leads to methodicalness, we infer that Oscar is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(oscar)</logic>
</step>

We now have Oscar as methodical, but the disjunction states "not methodical or knowledgeable". Since the first option is false (Oscar is methodical), the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the second option—Oscar being knowledgeable—to be true. Thus we infer his knowledge.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Since Oscar is methodical and the disjunction that either not methodical or knowledgeable holds, we can conclude that Oscar must be knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(oscar)</logic>
</step>

Having established Oscar's knowledge, we apply the earlier rule that knowledge forces cooperativeness. This yields Oscar as cooperative, a crucial fact for later negating decisiveness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Because Oscar is knowledgeable and knowledge implies cooperativeness, we deduce that Oscar is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(oscar)</logic>
</step>

With Oscar shown to be cooperative, we look at the rule that decisiveness would make someone not cooperative. If Oscar were decisive, he would have to be not cooperative, contradicting the cooperative status we just derived. Therefore Oscar cannot be decisive; we infer his non‑decisiveness.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Given Oscar is cooperative and the rule that decisiveness would make him not cooperative, we infer that Oscar cannot be decisive.</text>
<logic>~decisive(oscar)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Oscar is not decisive, the rule linking non‑decisiveness to patience kicks in, allowing us to conclude that Oscar is patient. This directly addresses the query about his lack of patience.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>With Oscar not being decisive and the rule that lack of decisiveness leads to patience, we conclude that Oscar is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(oscar)</logic>
</step>

We derived Oscar is patient, so the conclusion Oscar is not patient is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the martial arts academy, sensei_nina is known to be skillful. Any martial artist who is skillful is also nervous. Whenever someone is nervous, they become disciplined in their training. Moreover, either sensei_nina is not disciplined, or she is flexible.
examples.question: Is it true that sensei_nina is not flexible?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether sensei_nina is not flexible. To answer this we will follow the chain of information given about her abilities and the logical connections among them.

The first rule tells us that any skillful person is also nervous. This connection will let us turn the fact that sensei_nina is skillful into a statement about her nervousness later on. It sets up the first implication we will need.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Being skillful implies being nervous.</text>
<logic>nervous(sensei_nina) :- skillful(sensei_nina)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that nervousness leads to being disciplined. Once we have established that sensei_nina is nervous, this rule will allow us to conclude she is disciplined. It provides the second link in the reasoning chain.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Being nervous implies being disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(sensei_nina) :- nervous(sensei_nina)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction says that either sensei_nina is not disciplined or she is flexible. This alternative will become crucial after we derive her disciplined status, because it will let us eliminate the first option and infer the second.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either sensei_nina is not disciplined, or she is flexible.</text>
<logic>~disciplined(sensei_nina) v flexible(sensei_nina)</logic>
</step>

The fact that sensei_nina is skillful is our starting factual premise. It supplies the concrete property that will trigger the first rule, allowing us to move from a known fact to a derived one.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Sensei_nina is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(sensei_nina)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that sensei_nina is skillful together with the rule that skillful implies nervous, we can conclude she is nervous. This application of modus ponens transforms the known skillfulness into nervousness, preparing us for the next implication.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From her skillfulness and the first rule we infer that sensei_nina is nervous.</text>
<logic>nervous(sensei_nina)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know sensei_nina is nervous, we apply the second rule—nervous implies disciplined—to infer that she is disciplined. This step again uses modus ponens, turning the newly derived nervousness into disciplined status.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since she is nervous, the second rule gives that she is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(sensei_nina)</logic>
</step>

We have established that sensei_nina is disciplined, which directly contradicts the first alternative in the disjunction (not disciplined). Because a disjunction is true when at least one option holds, the falsity of the first option forces the second option to be true, so we conclude she is flexible. This is an instance of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Because she is disciplined, the disjunction forces her to be flexible.</text>
<logic>flexible(sensei_nina)</logic>
</step>

We derived Sensei_nina is flexible, so the conclusion Sensei_nina is not flexible is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: The protein bar is vitamin-rich. Whenever a protein bar is protein-dense, it is antioxidant. Whenever a protein bar is high-energy, it is not antioxidant. Either the protein bar is not rich in fiber or it is protein-dense. Either the protein bar is not vitamin-rich or it is rich in fiber.
examples.question: Is the protein bar not high-energy?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the protein bar lacks the high‑energy property. The goal is to derive a statement that the bar is not high‑energy using the information provided.

The rule linking protein density to antioxidant status will become useful once we know the bar is protein‑dense. It sets up a forward implication that we can later apply with Modus Ponens.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the protein bar is protein-dense, then it is antioxidant.</text>
<logic>antioxidant(protein_bar) :- protein_dense(protein_bar)</logic>
</step>

This rule tells us that high energy contradicts being antioxidant. It will allow us to turn an established antioxidant fact into a denial of high energy via Modus Tollens later on.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the protein bar is high-energy, then it is not antioxidant.</text>
<logic>~antioxidant(protein_bar) :- high_energy(protein_bar)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a choice: either the bar lacks fiber richness or it is protein‑dense. When we later learn the bar is fiber‑rich, this clause will force the protein‑dense alternative.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the protein bar is not rich in fiber, or it is protein-dense.</text>
<logic>~rich_in_fiber(protein_bar) v protein_dense(protein_bar)</logic>
</step>

Here we have another choice: either the bar is not vitamin‑rich or it is fiber‑rich. Since we already have the fact that the bar is vitamin‑rich, this clause will let us infer fiber richness via Disjunctive Syllogism.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the protein bar is not vitamin-rich, or it is rich in fiber.</text>
<logic>~vitamin_rich(protein_bar) v rich_in_fiber(protein_bar)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the bar is vitamin‑rich is our starting point. It directly activates the second disjunction, enabling us to derive additional properties.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The protein bar is vitamin-rich.</text>
<logic>vitamin_rich(protein_bar)</logic>
</step>

Applying Disjunctive Syllogism to the fact that the bar is vitamin‑rich and the clause "not vitamin‑rich or rich in fiber" eliminates the first option, leaving us with the conclusion that the bar is rich in fiber. This step converts a generic alternative into a concrete property.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,4">
<text>From the fact that the bar is vitamin-rich and the disjunction that a non‑vitamin‑rich bar would be rich in fiber, we infer that the bar is rich in fiber.</text>
<logic>rich_in_fiber(protein_bar)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the bar is rich in fiber, we revisit the earlier disjunction "not rich in fiber or protein‑dense". Since the bar is indeed rich in fiber, the first alternative is false, so the only remaining possibility is that the bar is protein‑dense. This gives us the needed protein‑dense fact.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since the bar is rich in fiber and we have the alternative that either it is not rich in fiber or it is protein-dense, we conclude the bar is protein-dense.</text>
<logic>protein_dense(protein_bar)</logic>
</step>

With the protein‑dense fact established, we can invoke the first rule: protein‑dense implies antioxidant. By Modus Ponens we infer that the bar is antioxidant, adding a key piece for the final contradiction.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Because the bar is protein-dense and protein-dense guarantees antioxidant, the bar is antioxidant.</text>
<logic>antioxidant(protein_bar)</logic>
</step>

We now have the bar being antioxidant, and the second rule tells us that any high‑energy bar would be non‑antioxidant. Using Modus Tollens, the presence of antioxidant forces the negation of high energy, yielding that the bar is not high‑energy.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Given the bar is antioxidant and the rule that any high-energy bar cannot be antioxidant, we deduce the bar is not high-energy.</text>
<logic>~high_energy(protein_bar)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Protein_bar is not high energy, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

<logic>strategic(player_one)</logic>
</step>

Because we have confirmed that player_one is strategic, the "not strategic" side of the disjunction is false. By disjunctive syllogism, the remaining side must be true, so player_one is hardworking. This gives us the concrete fact we need for the next inference.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Because player_one is strategic, the alternative "not strategic" in the disjunction is false, so player_one must be hardworking.</text>
<logic>hardworking(player_one)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know player_one is hardworking, we can apply the first rule (hardworking implies methodical). Using modus ponens, we deduce that player_one is methodical. This new fact puts us in a position to test the eager condition.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since player_one is hardworking and hardworking implies methodical, we conclude that player_one is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(player_one)</logic>
</step>

With player_one established as methodical, we look at the second rule, which says that eagerness would make a character not methodical. By modus tollens, the contrapositive tells us that if a character is methodical, they cannot be eager. Hence, player_one is not eager.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Because player_one is methodical, and being eager would make a character not methodical, we infer that player_one is not eager.</text>
<logic>~eager(player_one)</logic>
</step>

We derived Player_one is not eager, so the conclusion Player_one is eager is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Sam is strong. If Sam is strong, then Sam is active. If Sam is active, then Sam is productive. If Sam is productive, then Sam is perceptive. If Sam is witty, then Sam is rational. If Sam is rational, then Sam is punctual. If Sam is grateful, then Sam is not perceptive. Moreover, Sam is either grateful or witty.
examples.question: Is Sam punctual?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Sam is punctual. The goal is to chain the given facts and conditional statements until we can assert Sam's punctuality.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Sam is strong then Sam is active.</text>
<logic>active(sam) :- strong(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Sam is productive then Sam is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(sam) :- productive(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Sam is active then Sam is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(sam) :- active(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Sam is rational then Sam is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(sam) :- rational(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Sam is witty then Sam is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(sam) :- witty(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Sam is grateful then Sam is not perceptive.</text>
<logic>~perceptive(sam) :- grateful(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction that Sam is either grateful or witty.</text>
<logic>grateful(sam) v witty(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Sam is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(sam)</logic>
</step>

We start with the explicit fact that Sam is strong. The first rule tells us that any strong individual is also active, so we can apply modus ponens to obtain Sam's activity. This gives us the first new piece of information needed for later steps.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From Sam being strong and the rule that strength implies activity, we conclude Sam is active.</text>
<logic>active(sam)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Sam is active, we look at the rule linking activity to productivity. The rule states that activity guarantees productivity, so by again using modus ponens we infer that Sam is productive. This bridges the gap between strength and the later perceptiveness rule.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Using Sam's activity together with the rule that activity leads to productivity, we infer Sam is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(sam)</logic>
</step>

Having established Sam's productivity, we turn to the rule that productivity leads to perceptiveness. Applying modus ponens once more yields that Sam is perceptive. This fact will later allow us to eliminate the possibility that Sam is grateful.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Since Sam is productive, the rule linking productivity to perceptiveness lets us deduce Sam is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(sam)</logic>
</step>

The rule about gratitude says that if Sam were grateful, he would not be perceptive. Since we have just shown Sam is perceptive, the antecedent of that rule (Sam being grateful) must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Therefore we conclude Sam is not grateful, using modus tollens.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Because Sam is perceptive, and the rule that gratitude would make him not perceptive, we can infer Sam is not grateful.</text>
<logic>~grateful(sam)</logic>
</step>

We are left with the disjunction that Sam is either grateful or witty. Because we have just derived that Sam is not grateful, the only remaining option that satisfies the disjunction is that Sam is witty. This follows by disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Given that Sam is not grateful and that he must be either grateful or witty, we resolve the disjunction in favor of Sam being witty.</text>
<logic>witty(sam)</logic>
</step>

With Sam identified as witty, we can invoke the rule that wit implies rationality. Modus ponens gives us that Sam is rational, moving us closer to the target property of punctuality.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,5">
<text>From Sam being witty and the rule that wit yields rationality, we deduce Sam is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(sam)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule connecting rationality to punctuality tells us that any rational individual is punctual. Since we have established Sam's rationality, we apply modus ponens one last time to deduce that Sam is punctual.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">
<text>Finally, Sam's rationality together with the rule that rationality implies punctuality gives us Sam is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(sam)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Sam is punctual, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Anna is brave. Whenever Anna is not fair‑minded, she is strategic. If Anna is fair‑minded, then she is not fit. If Anna is focused, then she is not strategic. Either Anna is not brave or she is clear‑headed. Either Anna is not clear‑headed or she is fit.
examples.question: Is Anna focused?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a set of facts and conditional statements about Anna's qualities. The goal is to determine whether Anna can be considered focused based on the information provided.

This premise tells us how strategic status depends on the absence of fair‑mindedness. It will become useful once we know whether Anna is fair‑minded or not.

Because we know Anna is brave, the left side of the disjunction (~brave) is false, leaving clear‑headed as the only remaining option. Thus the disjunction forces the conclusion that Anna is clear‑headed.

<text>From Anna being brave and the first disjunction we infer that Anna is clear‑headed.</text>

Having just derived that Anna is clear‑headed, we turn to the second disjunction. The left side (~clear_headed) is now false, so the only way for the disjunction to hold is for Anna to be fit, which we therefore infer.

The rule from premise 2 tells us that if Anna were fair‑minded she would not be fit. Since we have established that she is fit, the antecedent (fair‑minded) must be false; otherwise the rule would be violated. Hence Anna cannot be fair‑minded.

<text>From Anna not being fair‑minded and the rule linking non‑fair‑mindedness to strategic, we infer that Anna is strategic.</text>

Premise 3 states that if Anna were focused she would not be strategic. We now know from step 10 that she is strategic, which contradicts the consequence of being focused. Therefore the antecedent (focused) must be false, giving us that Anna is not focused.

examples.context: Alan, Helen, Kurt and Phoenix are all robots. Each of them is known to be cheerful, so Alan is cheerful, Helen is cheerful, Kurt is cheerful, and Phoenix is cheerful. In addition, Alan, Kurt and Phoenix are rational, meaning Alan is rational, Kurt is rational, and Phoenix is rational. The system also contains three conditional rules that apply specifically to Helen: whenever Helen is rational she is also decisive, whenever Helen is rested she is rational, and whenever Helen is cheerful she is rested. These rules together link Helen's cheerfulness to her being rested, her restedness to rationality, and her rationality to decisiveness.
examples.question: Is Helen decisive?
examples.completion:
<think>

The fact that Helen is cheerful is the only concrete piece of information about her in the premises. It serves as the starting point for applying the conditional rules that follow.

Now that we know Helen is rested, the rested‑to‑rational rule lets us infer that Helen is rational. This moves us one step closer to the decisive conclusion by establishing the necessary rationality.

examples.context: Gail is ambitious, Dean is judicious, and Victor is sharp. Whenever Victor is sharp, he is also creative. If someone is vigorous, then they are diligent; thus, if Dean were vigorous, Dean would be diligent. If Victor is meticulous, then Victor is observant. Any person who is judicious is automatically studious, so Dean being judicious makes him studious. If Gail is reflective, then she is not ambitious. Either Gail is reflective or Gail is efficient. Either Victor is not creative or Victor is meticulous. Either Dean is not studious or Dean is vigorous.
examples.question: Is Gail efficient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Gail possesses the property of being efficient. The available information includes several facts about the characters and a collection of conditional and disjunctive statements that we can manipulate to reach a conclusion.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Victor is sharp, then Victor is creative.</text>
<logic>~ambitious(gail) :- reflective(gail)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Dean is vigorous, then Dean is diligent.</text>
<logic>reflective(gail) v efficient(gail)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Victor is meticulous, then Victor is observant.</text>
<logic>ambitious(gail)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>By Modus Tollens, the consequent is false, so the antecedent must be false: Gail is not reflective.</text>
<logic>~reflective(gail)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>By Disjunctive Syllogism, one disjunct is eliminated, leaving Gail is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(gail)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Gail is efficient, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Sprinter_lee is aggressive in play. Whenever an athlete is aggressive in play, they are also strategic. If an athlete is strategic, then they become steady. Whenever an athlete is steady, they are a quick thinker. Either sprinter_lee is not a quick thinker, or sprinter_lee is recovered.
examples.question: Is sprinter_lee recovered?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several pieces of information about sprinter_lee's performance traits and a logical relationship among them. Our goal is to determine whether sprinter_lee can be said to be recovered based on these facts and rules.

The first rule tells us that aggressiveness in play guarantees strategic ability. This will let us move from the known aggressive fact to a strategic conclusion later on.

The second rule links steadiness to quick thinking. It will become useful once we have established that sprinter_lee is steady, allowing us to infer quick thinking.

<logic>quick_thinker(sprinter_lee) :- steady(sprinter_lee)</logic>

The third rule says that being strategic leads to steadiness. This creates a chain: aggressive → strategic → steady, which we will exploit in the proof.

</step>

The disjunction gives us two possibilities: either sprinter_lee is not a quick thinker, or sprinter_lee is recovered. This will be the final lever to obtain the recovered status once we know the quick thinker status.

We are directly told that sprinter_lee is aggressive in play, providing the starting fact for the chain of implications.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The athlete is aggressive in play.</text>

</step>

Using the fact that sprinter_lee is aggressive and the rule that aggressiveness implies strategic, we can confidently assert that sprinter_lee is strategic. This follows the standard modus ponens pattern.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From the athlete's aggressiveness and the rule linking aggressiveness to strategy, we infer that the athlete is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(sprinter_lee)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established sprinter_lee is strategic, we apply the rule that strategic athletes become steady. Again, modus ponens lets us conclude that sprinter_lee is steady.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">

<logic>steady(sprinter_lee)</logic>

With sprinter_lee confirmed as steady, the rule connecting steadiness to quick thinking allows us to infer that sprinter_lee is a quick thinker. This step completes the forward chain needed for the disjunction.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Given the athlete is steady and the rule that steady leads to quick thinking, we deduce the athlete is a quick thinker.</text>
<logic>quick_thinker(sprinter_lee)</logic>

We now know sprinter_lee is a quick thinker. The disjunction states that either sprinter_lee is not a quick thinker or sprinter_lee is recovered. Since the first alternative is false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that sprinter_lee is recovered. This is a direct application of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Because the athlete is a quick thinker and the disjunction stating that either not quick thinker or recovered holds, we resolve that the athlete must be recovered.</text>
<logic>recovered(sprinter_lee)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Sprinter_lee is recovered, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: Cypress is known to be healthy. Whenever Cypress is cautious, Cypress is disciplined. Whenever Cypress is disciplined, Cypress becomes industrious. If Cypress were enthusiastic, then Cypress would not be capable. If Cypress were empathetic, then Cypress would not be productive. Either Cypress is empathetic or Cypress is cautious. Either Cypress is not healthy or Cypress is productive. Either Cypress is not industrious or Cypress is capable.
examples.question: Is Cypress enthusiastic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the ancient cedar known as Cypress can be described as enthusiastic. The goal is to see if the premises and logical consequences force enthusiasm to be impossible for Cypress.

This rule links cautiousness to disciplined behavior, which will become useful once we know whether Cypress is cautious. It sets up a conditional chain that later steps will exploit.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Cypress is cautious, then Cypress is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(cypress) :- cautious(cypress)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a second conditional that upgrades disciplined status to industriousness. Together with PREMISE_1 it creates a pathway from cautious to industrious.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Cypress is disciplined, then Cypress is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(cypress) :- disciplined(cypress)</logic>
</step>

This conditional tells us that enthusiasm would deny the capability property. It will later allow us to turn a derived capability into a negation of enthusiasm.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Cypress is enthusiastic, then Cypress is not capable.</text>
<logic>~capable(cypress) :- enthusiastic(cypress)</logic>
</step>

This rule says that if Cypress were empathetic, it could not be productive. It will be used to eliminate empathy once we have productivity.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Cypress is empathetic, then Cypress is not productive.</text>
<logic>~productive(cypress) :- empathetic(cypress)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives for Cypress: either it is empathetic or it is cautious. Knowing one of these will let us infer the other by elimination.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Cypress is either empathetic or cautious.</text>
<logic>empathetic(cypress) v cautious(cypress)</logic>
</step>

This disjunction provides a link between health and productivity. Because we already have a health fact, we can resolve this to obtain productivity.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Cypress is either not healthy or productive.</text>
<logic>~healthy(cypress) v productive(cypress)</logic>
</step>

The final disjunction connects industriousness with capability. Once we establish industriousness, this will force capability.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Cypress is either not industrious or capable.</text>
<logic>~industrious(cypress) v capable(cypress)</logic>
</step>

The sole factual premise states that Cypress is healthy, which will be paired with PREMISE_6 to derive productivity.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Cypress is healthy.</text>
<logic>healthy(cypress)</logic>
</step>

We apply disjunctive syllogism: the disjunction says "not healthy or productive". Since we know Cypress is healthy, the "not healthy" option is false, leaving productivity as the only true alternative. Thus Cypress is productive.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">
<text>From Cypress being healthy and the claim that either Cypress is not healthy or productive, we infer that Cypress must be productive.</text>
<logic>productive(cypress)</logic>
</step>

Using modus tollens, we notice that if Cypress were empathetic, it would not be productive. But we have just shown Cypress is productive, so the antecedent (empathetic) must be false. Hence Cypress is not empathetic.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Since Cypress is productive and if Cypress were empathetic then Cypress would not be productive, we conclude that Cypress cannot be empathetic.</text>
<logic>~empathetic(cypress)</logic>
</step>

Now we have a disjunction "empathetic or cautious" and we have just ruled out empathy. Disjunctive syllogism lets us conclude that the remaining option, cautious, must hold for Cypress.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Given that Cypress is not empathetic and that Cypress is either empathetic or cautious, the only remaining option is that Cypress is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(cypress)</logic>
</step>

With Cypress established as cautious, we invoke the conditional from PREMISE_1. Modus ponens tells us that cautiousness guarantees disciplined status, so Cypress becomes disciplined.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>With Cypress being cautious and the rule that cautiousness implies disciplined, we deduce that Cypress is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(cypress)</logic>
</step>

Having disciplined Cypress, we turn to PREMISE_2. Modus ponens again yields that a disciplined entity is industrious, so Cypress is industrious.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>Because Cypress is disciplined and disciplinedness leads to industriousness, Cypress is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(cypress)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction in PREMISE_7 says "not industrious or capable". Since we now know Cypress is industrious, the "not industrious" part is false, forcing the other disjunct to be true: Cypress is capable.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,7">
<text>Cypress is industrious, and the disjunction stating that either Cypress is not industrious or capable forces Cypress to be capable.</text>
<logic>capable(cypress)</logic>
</step>

Finally we use the conditional from PREMISE_3. Modus tollens says that if enthusiasm implied not capable, then having capability means enthusiasm cannot hold. Because Cypress is capable, we infer that Cypress is not enthusiastic.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,3">
<text>Cypress is capable, and the rule that enthusiasm would make Cypress not capable allows us to infer that Cypress cannot be enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>~enthusiastic(cypress)</logic>
</step>

We derived Cypress is not enthusiastic, so the conclusion Cypress is enthusiastic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The dietitian is goal_oriented.</text>
<logic>goal_oriented(dietitian)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule from Premise 3 to the fact in Premise 6, we see that a goal_oriented dietitian must be logical. Hence we can assert that the dietitian is logical.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>From the fact that the dietitian is goal_oriented and the rule linking goal_oriented to logical, we infer that the dietitian is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(dietitian)</logic>
</step>

Now we have logical as true, which contradicts the first disjunct of Premise 5. Because a disjunction is true when at least one part holds, the falsity of "not logical" forces the other part, "well_informed", to be true. Thus we obtain well_informedness.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Using the disjunction that the dietitian is not logical or well_informed together with the newly derived logical status, we conclude that the dietitian must be well_informed.</text>
<logic>well_informed(dietitian)</logic>
</step>

With well_informed established, Premise 2 tells us that well_informed implies attentiveness. Therefore the dietitian is attentive.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Because the dietitian is well_informed and the rule that well_informed implies attentive, we deduce that the dietitian is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(dietitian)</logic>
</step>

Since the dietitian is attentive, the consequent of Premise 4 ("not attentive") cannot occur. If she were self_assured, Premise 4 would make her not attentive, contradicting what we just derived. Consequently, we must conclude that she is not self_assured.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Given that the dietitian is attentive and the rule that self_assured would make her not attentive, we infer that she cannot be self_assured; therefore she is not self_assured.</text>
<logic>~self_assured(dietitian)</logic>
</step>

Finally, Premise 1 says that not being self_assured leads to being balanced. Having just shown the dietitian is not self_assured, we can directly infer that she is balanced.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>Since the dietitian is not self_assured and the rule that not self_assured leads to being balanced, we conclude that the dietitian is balanced.</text>
<logic>balanced(dietitian)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Dietitian is balanced, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the wildlife reserve, the tiger is impartial. The elephant feels cheerful. Any impartial tiger is agile, so if a tiger is impartial it becomes agile. If a tiger is knowledgeable, then it is dedicated. Whenever an elephant is cheerful, it becomes proactive. Whenever an elephant is rested, it turns productive. If an elephant is proactive, it is also rested. Moreover, the tiger is either not agile or it is knowledgeable. Finally, the elephant is either not productive or it is active.
examples.question: Is the elephant active?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the elephant in the reserve is active. To answer this, we will chain together the given facts and rules, using the disjunctions when needed, to see if active can be derived.

The first premise tells us that impartiality in the tiger guarantees agility. This will be useful later if we need to reason about the tiger's properties, though it does not directly affect the elephant.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any impartial tiger is agile.</text>
<logic>proactive(elephant) :- cheerful(elephant)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links knowledgeability to dedication for the tiger. Like the first, it concerns the tiger and may be used when handling the tiger's disjunction, but it is not immediately relevant to the elephant's activity.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any knowledgeable tiger is dedicated.</text>
<logic>productive(elephant) :- rested(elephant)</logic>
</step>

The third premise connects cheerfulness with proactivity for the elephant. Since we already know the elephant is cheerful, this rule will let us infer that the elephant is proactive, a key step toward establishing its activity.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any cheerful elephant is proactive.</text>
<logic>rested(elephant) :- proactive(elephant)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise says that if the elephant is rested, then it becomes productive. This conditional will become important after we later deduce that the elephant is rested.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any rested elephant is productive.</text>
<logic>~productive(elephant) v active(elephant)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise gives the reverse direction: a proactive elephant is also rested. Together with premise 3, it creates a chain from cheerfulness to proactivity to restedness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any proactive elephant is rested.</text>
<logic>cheerful(elephant)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Elephant is proactive.</text>
<logic>proactive(elephant)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Elephant is rested.</text>
<logic>rested(elephant)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Elephant is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(elephant)</logic>
</step>

Because the elephant is cheerful (Premise 8) and the rule that cheerful elephants are proactive (Premise 3), we can conclude the elephant is proactive. This is a direct application of the conditional, moving us one step closer to activity.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>From the elephant being cheerful and the rule linking cheerfulness to proactivity, we infer that the elephant is proactive.</text>
<logic>active(elephant)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Elephant is active, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Patient Jamie is known to be compliant. Whenever a patient receives experimental therapy, they become responsive to treatment. If a patient is responsive to treatment, they follow the prescription. If a patient has stable vitals, then they are not compliant. The patient either has stable vitals or receives experimental therapy.
examples.question: Does patient_jamie follow the prescription?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether patient_jamie follows the prescription. The goal is to trace the logical connections from the known facts and rules to reach a conclusion about the prescription‑following behavior.

The first rule links experimental therapy to treatment responsiveness, which will be useful later when we establish that the patient indeed receives experimental therapy. Recognizing this conditional relationship sets up a path to derive responsiveness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if a patient receives experimental therapy, then they become responsive to treatment.</text>
<logic>responsive_to_treatment(patient_jamie) :- experimental_therapy(patient_jamie)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that responsiveness to treatment guarantees that the patient follows the prescription. This rule is the final bridge we need to connect responsiveness to the desired conclusion about prescription adherence.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any patient who is responsive to treatment will follow the prescription.</text>
<logic>follows_prescription(patient_jamie) :- responsive_to_treatment(patient_jamie)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a way to contradict the patient’s compliance if stable vitals were present. By linking stable vitals to non‑compliance, it creates an opportunity to eliminate the stable‑vitals possibility later on.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that if a patient has stable vitals, then they are not compliant.</text>
<logic>~compliant(patient_jamie) :- stable_vitals(patient_jamie)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that exactly one of two conditions holds for the patient: either stable vitals or experimental therapy. This alternative will allow us to infer the experimental therapy once we rule out stable vitals.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that the patient either has stable vitals or receives experimental therapy.</text>
<logic>stable_vitals(patient_jamie) v experimental_therapy(patient_jamie)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that patient_jamie is compliant, establishing a factual anchor for the reasoning process. This fact will interact with the rule about stable vitals to produce a contradiction.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that patient Jamie is compliant.</text>
<logic>compliant(patient_jamie)</logic>
</step>

Since the patient is compliant, the rule that stable vitals imply non‑compliance cannot be true for this patient. Therefore, the assumption of stable vitals leads to a contradiction, allowing us to conclude that the patient does not have stable vitals. This negation is essential for applying the disjunction in the next step.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>From the fact that patient Jamie is compliant and the rule linking stable vitals to non‑compliance, we infer that patient Jamie cannot have stable vitals.</text>
<logic>~stable_vitals(patient_jamie)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that patient_jamie does not have stable vitals, the disjunction forces the other option to be true: the patient must receive experimental therapy. This step uses disjunctive syllogism to move from a negated alternative to a positive assertion.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Since patient Jamie does not have stable vitals and we know that either stable vitals or experimental therapy must hold, we conclude that patient Jamie receives experimental therapy.</text>
<logic>experimental_therapy(patient_jamie)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the patient receives experimental therapy, we can invoke the first rule, which says experimental therapy leads to responsiveness to treatment. By modus ponens, we derive that patient_jamie is responsive to treatment, bringing us closer to the final goal.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Because patient Jamie receives experimental therapy and the rule that experimental therapy implies responsiveness to treatment, we derive that patient Jamie is responsive to treatment.</text>
<logic>responsive_to_treatment(patient_jamie)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know patient_jamie is responsive to treatment, the second rule directly yields that the patient follows the prescription. Applying modus ponens again converts responsiveness into the desired prescription‑following behavior.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Given that patient Jamie is responsive to treatment and the rule that responsiveness leads to following the prescription, we infer that patient Jamie follows the prescription.</text>
<logic>follows_prescription(patient_jamie)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Patient_jamie is follows prescription, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the marine research team, Dara and Rio are both noted for being generous. Zion is recognized as optimistic, while West has shown a resilient attitude. Whenever something is trustworthy, it is also analytical, so if Zion were trustworthy, Zion would be analytical. Any optimistic entity automatically qualifies as fit, which means Zion being optimistic makes Zion fit. Generosity guarantees friendliness; consequently, Rio’s generosity ensures that Rio is friendly. Resilience leads to rationality, so West’s resilience implies West is rational. Impartiality brings relaxation, meaning that if West were impartial, West would be relaxed. Friendliness gives strength, therefore Rio’s friendliness would make Rio strong. Finally, being reflective prevents strength: if Rio were reflective, then Rio could not be strong. In addition to these rules, there are two exclusive alternatives: either Zion is not fit or Zion is trustworthy, and either West is not rational or West is impartial. These statements together capture all the information we have about the four oceanographers.

examples.completion:
<think>

This rule connects trustworthiness to analyticity for Zion, which will later interact with the disjunction involving Zion’s fit status. It sets up a conditional that may become relevant if we ever establish Zion’s trustworthiness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Zion is trustworthy, then Zion must also be analytical.</text>
<logic>friendly(rio) :- generous(rio)</logic>
</step>

Here, optimism guarantees fitness. Because Zion is explicitly optimistic, this rule will allow us to derive that Zion is fit, which will later satisfy one side of the first disjunction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Zion is optimistic, then Zion is fit according to the second rule.</text>
<logic>strong(rio) :- friendly(rio)</logic>
</step>

The generosity‑to‑friendliness rule tells us that any generous person, including Rio, must also be friendly. This will be the first stepping stone toward proving Rio’s strength.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Generous individuals are friendly, so Rio being generous implies Rio is friendly.</text>
<logic>~strong(rio) :- reflective(rio)</logic>
</step>

Resilience leading to rationality gives us a way to connect West’s resilient nature to a rational status. This will be useful when we examine the second disjunction about West.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Resilience implies rationality, therefore West's resilience gives West rationality.</text>
<logic>generous(rio)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>By Universal Instantiation, grounding the quantified rule for this entity gives Rio is friendly.</text>
<logic>friendly(rio)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">

<logic>strong(rio)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>By Modus Tollens, the consequent is false, so the antecedent must be false: Rio is not reflective.</text>
<logic>~reflective(rio)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Rio is not reflective, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Pearl is known to be calm. Whenever Pearl is observant, she becomes prepared. If someone is calm, then that person is principled, so Pearl’s calmness guarantees that she is principled. Being open‑minded would make Pearl not prepared; in other words, if Pearl were open‑minded, she could not be prepared. Finally, either Pearl is not principled or she is observant, giving a clear alternative about her principled and observant status.
examples.question: Is Pearl open‑minded?
examples.completion:
<think>
We start with a set of facts and conditional statements about Pearl’s mental traits. Our goal is to decide whether Pearl can be considered open‑minded based on the information given.

The first rule connects observance to preparedness, which will become useful once we know whether Pearl is observant. It tells us that establishing observance is enough to derive preparedness later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Pearl is observant then she is prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(pearl) :- observant(pearl)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links calmness to being principled, allowing us to move from the known fact that Pearl is calm to a new fact about her principled nature. This step is essential because the principled status will interact with the disjunction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that calmness guarantees principledness for Pearl.</text>
<logic>principled(pearl) :- calm(pearl)</logic>
</step>

The third rule gives a contrapositive relationship: if Pearl were open‑minded, she could not be prepared. This will let us later argue that because Pearl is prepared, she cannot be open‑minded.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that being open‑minded implies Pearl is not prepared.</text>
<logic>~prepared(pearl) :- open_minded(pearl)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives: either Pearl is not principled or she is observant. Once we establish one side, the other side can be ruled out, helping us to pinpoint Pearl’s observance.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction tells us that either Pearl is not principled or she is observant.</text>
<logic>~principled(pearl) v observant(pearl)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Pearl is calm, which is the starting factual anchor for the chain of deductions. This fact will activate the calm‑to‑principled rule.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>We are told directly that Pearl is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(pearl)</logic>
</step>

Applying the calm‑to‑principled rule to the fact that Pearl is calm yields that Pearl is principled. This deduction follows directly from modus ponens: the antecedent (calm) holds, so the consequent (principled) must hold as well.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>From Pearl’s calmness and the rule linking calm to principled, we infer that Pearl is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(pearl)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Pearl as principled, and the disjunction says either not principled or observant. Since the first option is false (Pearl is principled), the disjunction forces the second option, making Pearl observant. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Using the principled fact together with the disjunction, we deduce that Pearl must be observant.</text>
<logic>observant(pearl)</logic>
</step>

With Pearl confirmed as observant, we can invoke the observant‑to‑prepared rule. Because the antecedent (observant) is true, modus ponens gives us that Pearl is prepared.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Given Pearl is observant, the rule about observant leading to prepared lets us conclude Pearl is prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(pearl)</logic>
</step>

We now have Pearl prepared, and the rule linking open‑mindedness to not‑prepared tells us that if Pearl were open‑minded, she would have to be not prepared. Since she is prepared, the only way to satisfy the rule is for Pearl not to be open‑minded. This is an application of modus tollens.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since Pearl is prepared, the rule that open‑mindedness would make her not prepared lets us infer that Pearl cannot be open‑minded.</text>
<logic>~open_minded(pearl)</logic>
</step>

We derived Pearl is not open minded, so the conclusion Pearl is open minded is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: The blue whale is an expert_swimmer. Whenever the blue whale is well_equipped, it is adventurous. Being an expert_swimmer guarantees that the blue whale is well_equipped. Any adventurous blue whale is also hardy. Either the blue whale is not hardy, or it is peaceful.
examples.question: Is the blue whale peaceful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the blue whale is peaceful. To answer this, we need to chain together the facts and conditional statements provided about its abilities and traits.

The first rule links being well_equipped to being adventurous, so if we can show the whale is well_equipped, we will obtain adventurousness. This connection will later help us reach the hardy property.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever the blue whale is well_equipped, it is adventurous.</text>
<logic>adventurous(blue_whale) :- well_equipped(blue_whale)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that expert_swimmer status guarantees the whale is well_equipped. Since we already know the whale is an expert_swimmer, this rule is crucial for initiating the chain of deductions.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Being an expert_swimmer guarantees that the blue whale is well_equipped.</text>
<logic>well_equipped(blue_whale) :- expert_swimmer(blue_whale)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that any adventurous blue whale is also hardy. Once we have adventurousness, we can immediately infer hardiness, which is needed for the final disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Any adventurous blue whale is also hardy.</text>
<logic>hardy(blue_whale) :- adventurous(blue_whale)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction says that either the whale is not hardy or it is peaceful. If we can establish that the whale is indeed hardy, the first alternative becomes false, leaving peaceful as the only viable option.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the blue whale is not hardy, or it is peaceful.</text>
<logic>~hardy(blue_whale) v peaceful(blue_whale)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the blue whale is an expert_swimmer provides the starting point for the forward reasoning. It directly activates the rule that yields well_equipped status.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The blue whale is an expert_swimmer.</text>
<logic>expert_swimmer(blue_whale)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that the whale is an expert_swimmer and the rule that expert_swimmer implies well_equipped, we can conclude the whale is well_equipped. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Thus, the blue whale is well_equipped.</text>
<logic>well_equipped(blue_whale)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the whale is well_equipped, we can apply the rule linking well_equipped to adventurousness. Therefore, the whale becomes adventurous, again via modus ponens.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Therefore, the blue whale is adventurous.</text>
<logic>adventurous(blue_whale)</logic>
</step>

Now that the whale is adventurous, the rule that adventurousness leads to hardiness allows us to infer that the whale is hardy. This step completes the forward chain needed for the disjunction.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Consequently, the blue whale is hardy.</text>
<logic>hardy(blue_whale)</logic>
</step>

We now know the whale is hardy, which makes the left side of the disjunction (~hardy) false. In a disjunction, if one side is false, the other side must be true, so the whale must be peaceful. This is a direct use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Given that the blue whale is hardy, the disjunction forces that it must be peaceful.</text>
<logic>peaceful(blue_whale)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Blue_whale is peaceful, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the pottery studio, Tom works with a precise technique. The workshop notes state that Tom is either not precise or balanced. Any potter who is balanced is also seasoned, and any potter who is pliable is not seasoned. These statements together with the explicit fact that Tom is precise form the basis for our reasoning about his pliability.
examples.question: Is Tom pliable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether Tom possesses the quality of pliability. Our goal is to see if the premises force Tom to be not pliable, which would make the query false.

The first rule connects the property of being balanced with being seasoned. Knowing this link will later let us turn a balance conclusion into a seasoned fact about Tom.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if a potter is balanced, then that potter is seasoned; this applies to Tom.</text>
<logic>seasoned(tom) :- balanced(tom)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that pliability guarantees the opposite of seasoned. This inverse relationship will be crucial for deriving a negation of pliability once we have seasoned.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if a potter is pliable, then that potter is not seasoned; this also concerns Tom.</text>
<logic>~seasoned(tom) :- pliable(tom)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>According to the notes, Tom is either not precise or balanced.</text>
<logic>~precise(tom) v balanced(tom)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is a stated fact that Tom is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(tom)</logic>
</step>

We have a disjunction that Tom is either not precise or balanced, and we also know Tom is precise. Since the first part of the disjunction is false, the only way for the statement to hold is for the second part—Tom being balanced—to be true. Thus, we deduce that Tom is balanced.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Because Tom is precise and the disjunction says he is either not precise or balanced, we can discard the first option and conclude that Tom is balanced.</text>
<logic>balanced(tom)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Tom is balanced, we can apply the first rule: any balanced potter is seasoned. By substituting Tom into this rule, we conclude that Tom is seasoned.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since Tom is balanced and the rule links balanced to seasoned, we infer that Tom is seasoned.</text>
<logic>seasoned(tom)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Tom seasoned, but the second rule says any pliable potter cannot be seasoned. If Tom were pliable, he would have to be not seasoned, which contradicts the seasoned fact we just derived. Therefore, Tom cannot be pliable, giving us the negation of pliability.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given that Tom is seasoned and the rule linking pliable to not seasoned, we must conclude that Tom cannot be pliable, i.e., Tom is not pliable.</text>
<logic>~pliable(tom)</logic>
</step>

We derived Tom is not pliable, so the conclusion Tom is pliable is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Blake is prepared, and Haven is supportive. Whenever Blake is agile, Blake becomes content. Whenever Haven is supportive, Haven is impartial. If Blake is knowledgeable, then Blake is insightful. If Haven is clever, then Haven is principled. If Blake is experienced, then Blake is not content. At least one of the following holds for Blake: Blake is experienced or Blake is astute. Either Haven is not impartial or Haven is clever. Either Blake is not insightful or Blake is agile. Either Blake is not prepared or Blake is knowledgeable.
examples.question: Is Blake astute?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Blake possesses the quality of being astute. The premises give us a mixture of facts, conditional rules, and alternative possibilities that we can chain together to reach a conclusion about Blake's astuteness.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Blake is agile, then Blake is content.</text>
<logic>content(blake) :- agile(blake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Haven is supportive, then Haven is impartial.</text>
<logic>insightful(blake) :- knowledgeable(blake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Blake is knowledgeable, then Blake is insightful.</text>
<logic>~content(blake) :- experienced(blake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Haven is clever, then Haven is principled.</text>
<logic>experienced(blake) v astute(blake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Blake is experienced, then Blake is not content.</text>
<logic>~insightful(blake) v agile(blake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Blake is experienced or Blake is astute.</text>
<logic>~prepared(blake) v knowledgeable(blake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Haven is not impartial or Haven is clever.</text>
<logic>prepared(blake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,6">
<text>By Disjunctive Syllogism, one disjunct is eliminated, leaving Blake is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(blake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Blake is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(blake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>By Disjunctive Syllogism, one disjunct is eliminated, leaving Blake is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(blake)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that either Blake is not prepared or Blake is knowledgeable. Since we already know Blake is prepared, the first alternative cannot hold, forcing the second alternative to be true. Therefore Blake must be knowledgeable. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>From Blake being prepared and the disjunction that either Blake is not prepared or Blake is knowledgeable, we infer Blake is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>content(blake)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Blake is knowledgeable, we can apply the conditional rule that knowledge leads to insight. Because the antecedent (knowledgeable Blake) is satisfied, the consequent (insightful Blake) follows directly.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Knowing Blake is knowledgeable together with the rule that knowledge makes Blake insightful yields Blake is insightful.</text>
<logic>~experienced(blake)</logic>
</step>

We have Blake being insightful, and another disjunction tells us that either Blake is not insightful or Blake is agile. The first option is contradicted by our newly derived fact that Blake is insightful, so the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Blake is agile. Hence we infer agility.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Since Blake is insightful and the disjunction that either Blake is not insightful or Blake is agile holds, we deduce Blake is agile.</text>
<logic>astute(blake)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Blake is astute, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the bustling pottery studio, four artisans are known for their daring techniques. The glaze master works with bold strokes, the wheel enthusiast also displays boldness, the kiln keeper is bold in managing heat, and the shaper is equally bold in shaping clay. Moreover, whenever the glaze master shows boldness, this daring attitude guarantees that the glaze master becomes malleable in handling different materials. Being malleable, in turn, ensures that the glaze master behaves courteously toward fellow potters.
examples.question: Is the glaze master not courteous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the glaze master fails to be courteous. To answer this, we must examine the facts about the glaze master's boldness and the conditional rules that connect boldness, malleability, and courtesy.

The first rule tells us that boldness guarantees malleability for the glaze master. Recognizing this link is crucial because it allows us to move from a property we already know (bold) to another property (malleable) that will later give us courtesy.

<text>If the glaze master is bold, then the glaze master is malleable.</text>
<logic>malleable(glaze_master) :- bold(glaze_master)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that whenever the glaze master is malleable, courtesy follows. This provides the final bridge we need: once we have malleability, we can immediately infer courtesy.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the glaze master is malleable, then the glaze master is courteous.</text>
<logic>courteous(glaze_master) :- malleable(glaze_master)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that the glaze master is bold. This fact supplies the starting point for applying the first rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The glaze master is bold.</text>
<logic>bold(glaze_master)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the glaze master is bold together with the rule that boldness implies malleability, we can deduce that the glaze master is indeed malleable. This step follows the standard pattern of applying a conditional statement to a known antecedent.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>From the glaze master's boldness and the rule linking boldness to malleability, we infer that the glaze master is malleable.</text>
<logic>malleable(glaze_master)</logic>
</step>

Having established the glaze master's malleability, we now apply the second rule, which converts malleability into courtesy. Consequently, we conclude that the glaze master is courteous.

examples.context: In the river ecosystem, the otter is migratory and also photosynthetic. The beaver is migratory and photosynthetic as well. The river plant is photosynthetic. Moreover, being photosynthetic ensures that the river plant builds habitat.
examples.question: Is the river plant a keystone species?

We are asked to determine whether the river plant qualifies as a keystone species. To answer this, we must trace how the given properties of the river plant lead to the keystone status through the supplied ecological rules.

The first rule tells us that habitat-building behavior causes migration. Recognizing this link is essential because migration is a prerequisite for keystone status later in the chain.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the river plant builds habitat, then it migrates.</text>
<logic>migratory(river_plant) :- habitat_builder(river_plant)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects migration directly to being a keystone species. This provides the final bridge we need once we have established that the river plant migrates.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the river plant migrates, then it is a keystone species.</text>
<logic>keystone(river_plant) :- migratory(river_plant)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that photosynthetic ability guarantees habitat-building. Since we know the river plant is photosynthetic, this rule will allow us to derive the habitat-building property.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the river plant is photosynthetic, then it builds habitat.</text>
<logic>habitat_builder(river_plant) :- photosynthetic(river_plant)</logic>

The fact that the river plant is photosynthetic gives us a concrete starting point. It activates the previous rule about habitat building.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The river plant is photosynthetic.</text>
<logic>photosynthetic(river_plant)</logic>
</step>

Applying the third rule to the fact that the river plant is photosynthetic, we infer that the river plant builds habitat. This step uses universal instantiation to specialize the general rule to our specific river plant.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Because the river plant is photosynthetic, we can infer it builds habitat.</text>
<logic>habitat_builder(river_plant)</logic>
</step>

With the newly derived habitat-building property, we invoke the first rule. Because the river plant builds habitat, the rule tells us it must also be migratory. This is a straightforward modus ponens application.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">

<logic>migratory(river_plant)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the river plant migrates, we can use the second rule. Migration leads directly to keystone status, so we conclude the river plant is a keystone species.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Having established that the river plant migrates, we conclude it is a keystone species.</text>
<logic>keystone(river_plant)</logic>
</step>

Therefore River_plant is keystone, so the conclusion is True.

Answer: True

examples.context: Hector is insightful. Whenever Hector is judicious, he is scholarly. If Hector is experienced, then he is not scholarly. Either Hector is not insightful or he is well prepared. Either Hector is not well prepared or he is judicious.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Hector is not experienced?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Hector is not experienced. To do this we will use the given facts, conditional rules, and disjunctions to build a chain of reasoning that leads to the negation of the experienced predicate.

The first rule connects judiciousness to scholarship. Recognizing this link is crucial because later steps will give us judiciousness, allowing us to derive scholarly status, which will be needed for the final contradiction with the experienced rule.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Hector is judicious, then he must be scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(hector) :- judicious(hector)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that experience forces non‑scholarship. This converse relationship is essential: if we can ever show Hector is scholarly, the rule will let us infer that he cannot be experienced, achieving our goal.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Hector is experienced, then he cannot be scholarly.</text>
<logic>~scholarly(hector) :- experienced(hector)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction about insightfulness and preparedness gives us a logical alternative: either Hector lacks insight or he is well prepared. Since we already know Hector is insightful, this disjunction will let us eliminate the first option and obtain a positive fact about preparedness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The statement says that either Hector is not insightful or he is well prepared.</text>
<logic>~insightful(hector) v well_prepared(hector)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction links lack of preparedness to judiciousness. Once we have established that Hector is well prepared, this sentence will allow us to rule out the ‘not well prepared’ side and obtain judiciousness, which will feed into the scholarly rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The statement says that either Hector is not well prepared or he is judicious.</text>
<logic>~well_prepared(hector) v judicious(hector)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Hector is insightful provides the concrete premise needed to activate the first disjunction. Knowing he possesses insight lets us apply disjunctive syllogism to derive a new piece of information about his state.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Hector is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(hector)</logic>
</step>

We apply disjunctive syllogism: the disjunction says ‘not insightful or well prepared’. Because we have the fact that Hector is insightful, the ‘not insightful’ alternative cannot hold, so the only remaining possibility is that Hector is well prepared. This gives us a solid starting point for the next inference.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>From Hector being insightful and the fact that either he is not insightful or well prepared, we discard the impossible ‘not insightful’ option and infer that Hector is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(hector)</logic>
</step>

Now we use the second disjunction, which reads ‘not well prepared or judicious’. Since we have just derived that Hector is well prepared, the ‘not well prepared’ clause is false, leaving judicious as the only viable option. Hence, Hector is judged to be judicious.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Having established that Hector is well prepared and knowing that either he is not well prepared or judicious, we eliminate the ‘not well prepared’ alternative, concluding that Hector is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(hector)</logic>
</step>

With judiciousness established, we invoke the first rule (judicious → scholarly). The rule guarantees that any judicious individual is also scholarly, so Hector must be scholarly. This new fact is the bridge to the experienced rule.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since Hector is judicious and the rule tells us that judiciousness leads to scholarly status, we can infer that Hector is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(hector)</logic>
</step>

We now have Hector scholarly, but the rule concerning experience tells us that an experienced Hector would be not scholarly. By contraposition (modus tollens), if Hector is indeed scholarly, the antecedent ‘experienced’ must be false. Therefore, Hector is not experienced.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>We now have Hector scholarly, but the rule says that if he were experienced he would not be scholarly; therefore, by the contrapositive, Hector cannot be experienced.</text>
<logic>~experienced(hector)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Hector is not experienced, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In this chess scenario, the queen is aggressive. Whenever the queen is methodical, she is also strategic. If the queen is strategic, then she is not aggressive. These three statements provide all of the information we have about the queen.
examples.question: Is the queen methodical?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the queen possesses the property of being methodical. To answer this, we will use the given fact about the queen’s aggressiveness together with the two conditional rules linking methodical, strategic, and aggressiveness.

The first rule connects methodical behavior to strategic insight; it tells us that any queen who is methodical must also be strategic. This link will allow us to reason from a potential methodical status to a strategic one later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if the queen is methodical, then she is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(queen) :- methodical(queen)</logic>
</step>

The second rule provides a negative relationship: a strategic queen cannot be aggressive. This will be crucial because we already know the queen is aggressive, so the rule can be used to block the possibility of her being strategic.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if the queen is strategic, then she is not aggressive.</text>
<logic>~aggressive(queen) :- strategic(queen)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that the queen is aggressive gives us a concrete starting point. Since the rule in PREMISE_2 says strategic implies not aggressive, the presence of aggressiveness will let us apply modus tollens to deny strategic status.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The fact given is that the queen is aggressive.</text>
<logic>aggressive(queen)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus tollens, we observe that if the queen were strategic, she would have to be not aggressive, but we know she is aggressive. Hence the assumption that she is strategic leads to a contradiction, allowing us to infer that the queen is not strategic. This step eliminates the strategic possibility, setting up the next inference.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Because the queen is aggressive and a strategic queen would have to be not aggressive, we infer that the queen cannot be strategic.</text>
<logic>~strategic(queen)</logic>
</step>

Now we use the first rule in reverse: it states that methodical implies strategic. Since we have just shown the queen is not strategic, the only way for her to be methodical would be impossible, because a methodical queen would necessarily be strategic. Therefore, by another application of modus tollens, we conclude the queen is not methodical.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Since the queen cannot be strategic and a methodical queen would have to be strategic, we conclude that the queen cannot be methodical.</text>
<logic>~methodical(queen)</logic>
</step>

We derived Queen is not methodical, so the conclusion Queen is methodical is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Brock is judged to be judicious. Whenever someone is studious, they are calm, so if Brock were studious he would be calm. If someone is resourceful, they become cheerful; thus a resourceful Brock would be cheerful. Being judicious guarantees honesty, so a judicious Brock is honest. Any cheerful person is observant, meaning a cheerful Brock would be observant. If someone is impartial, they are resourceful; therefore an impartial Brock would be resourceful. Honesty leads to studiousness, so an honest Brock would be studious. Courageousness forces a lack of calm, i.e., if Brock were courageous then Brock would not be calm. Finally, either Brock is courageous or Brock is impartial. These are all the facts and rules that govern the situation.
examples.question: Is Brock observant?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Brock possesses the quality of being observant. To answer, we must follow the chain of implications provided in the context, starting from the known fact that Brock is judicious and moving forward until we can reach observant or determine it cannot be derived.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Brock is studious then Brock is calm.</text>

</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Brock is resourceful then Brock is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(brock) :- resourceful(brock)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">

</step>

</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
