<logic>resourceful(brock) :- impartial(brock)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Brock is honest then Brock is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(brock) :- honest(brock)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Brock is courageous then Brock is not calm.</text>
<logic>~calm(brock) :- courageous(brock)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that Brock is either courageous or impartial.</text>
<logic>courageous(brock) v impartial(brock)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Brock is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(brock)</logic>
</step>

The context tells us that any judicious individual is honest. Since we already know Brock is judicious, we can directly apply this rule and conclude that Brock is honest. This gives us a new fact to work with in the next steps.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>From Brock being judicious we infer that Brock is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(brock)</logic>

Now that we have established Brock's honesty, we can use the rule that honesty leads to studiousness. By applying this implication to Brock, we obtain that Brock is studious, adding another link in our reasoning chain.

With Brock being studious, the rule linking studiousness to calmness allows us to infer that Brock is calm. This fact will be crucial for the subsequent use of the contrapositive reasoning involving courage.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">

<logic>calm(brock)</logic>
</step>

We also know that if Brock were courageous, he would not be calm. Since we have just derived that Brock is calm, the only way to keep the conditional true is for Brock not to be courageous. This contrapositive step lets us assert that Brock is not courageous.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Since Brock is calm, the conditional that courageous implies not calm lets us conclude Brock is not courageous.</text>
<logic>~courageous(brock)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction in the premises says Brock is either courageous or impartial. Having shown that Brock cannot be courageous, the only remaining possibility is that he must be impartial. Thus we conclude Brock is impartial.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">
<text>Because Brock is not courageous and we know he is either courageous or impartial, we must conclude Brock is impartial.</text>
<logic>impartial(brock)</logic>

Given Brock's impartiality, we can apply the rule that impartial individuals are resourceful. This yields the new fact that Brock is resourceful, moving us closer to the target property.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">

<logic>resourceful(brock)</logic>

From Brock being resourceful, the rule stating that resourceful people are cheerful lets us deduce that Brock is cheerful. This is the immediate predecessor of observant in the rule set.

examples.context: In the wildlife reserve, the plant clover has been noted as decisive. Whenever something is decisive, it is clear-headed, so clover is clear-headed. Being clear-headed guarantees that clover is thorough, and any thorough clover is also analytical. However, if clover were open-minded, it would not be analytical. Finally, either clover is open-minded or clover is reflective.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that clover is reflective?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the plant clover can be shown to be reflective using the supplied facts and rules. The goal is to link the known decisive nature of clover all the way to the reflective property through a chain of logical implications.

The first rule tells us that thoroughness leads to analyticity for clover. This connection will become important later when we establish that clover is thorough, allowing us to infer analyticity.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If clover is thorough, then it is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(clover) :- thorough(clover)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links decisiveness to being clear‑headed. Since we already know clover is decisive, this rule gives us a direct route to obtain the clear‑headed attribute.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If clover is decisive, then it is clear-headed.</text>
<logic>clear_headed(clover) :- decisive(clover)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that clear‑headedness guarantees thoroughness. Once we have clear‑headedness, we can move forward to claim that clover is thorough, which is needed for the next step.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If clover is clear-headed, then it is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(clover) :- clear_headed(clover)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule provides a negative relationship: if clover were open‑minded, it could not be analytical. This will be used later to eliminate the possibility of clover being open‑minded after we have shown it is analytical.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If clover is open-minded, then it is not analytical.</text>
<logic>~analytical(clover) :- open_minded(clover)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: clover is either open‑minded or reflective. This binary choice will allow us to conclude reflectiveness once we rule out the open‑minded option.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either clover is open-minded or clover is reflective.</text>
<logic>open_minded(clover) v reflective(clover)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that clover is decisive, giving us a solid starting fact for the chain of deductions that follow.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Clover is decisive.</text>
<logic>decisive(clover)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule that decisiveness implies clear‑headedness to the fact that clover is decisive, we conclude that clover must be clear‑headed. This creates the bridge to the next rule about thoroughness.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From clover being decisive we infer that clover is clear-headed.</text>
<logic>clear_headed(clover)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established clover is clear‑headed, we can use the rule linking clear‑headedness to thoroughness. Consequently, clover is determined to be thorough, setting up the condition needed for analytical status.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>From clover being clear-headed we infer that clover is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(clover)</logic>
</step>

With clover confirmed as thorough, the first rule tells us that thoroughness entails analyticity. Therefore, clover is analytical, which will be crucial for negating the open‑minded possibility.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From clover being thorough we infer that clover is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(clover)</logic>
</step>

Since we have derived that clover is analytical, the fourth rule tells us that an open‑minded clover would have to be non‑analytical. This creates a contradiction, so we infer that clover cannot be open‑minded; thus, ~open_minded(clover) holds.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Since clover is analytical and open-minded would make it non‑analytical, we conclude that clover is not open-minded.</text>
<logic>~open_minded(clover)</logic>
</step>

We now have ~open_minded(clover) together with the disjunction that clover is either open‑minded or reflective. Because the open‑minded alternative is ruled out, the only remaining possibility is that clover is reflective.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Given that clover is not open-minded and that either it is open-minded or reflective, we deduce that clover is reflective.</text>
<logic>reflective(clover)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Clover is reflective, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the garden, the fern is known to be agile. Whenever a plant is agile, it is also grateful. Any grateful plant becomes brilliant. Being brilliant guarantees that the plant is supportive. If a plant is scholarly, then it cannot be supportive. The fern will be active whenever it is responsible. Finally, the fern satisfies a disjunction: it is either scholarly or responsible. These statements capture all the facts, rules, and the lone disjunction relevant to the problem.
examples.question: Is it true that the fern is not active?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the fern fails to be active. To answer, we will follow the chain of given rules and facts, trying to see if we can establish the fern’s activity status.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if the fern is responsible then it is active.</text>
<logic>active(fern) :- responsible(fern)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if the fern is grateful then it is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(fern) :- grateful(fern)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">

</step>

<logic>~supportive(fern) :- scholarly(fern)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The statement that the fern is either scholarly or responsible.</text>
<logic>scholarly(fern) v responsible(fern)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The fact that the fern is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(fern)</logic>
</step>

The only explicit fact we have is that the fern is agile. One of the rules tells us that any agile entity is also grateful. By applying this rule to the fern, we can safely assert that the fern is grateful. This gives us a new piece of information to use in the next inference.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>From the fern’s agility and the rule linking agility to gratitude, we infer that the fern is grateful.</text>
<logic>grateful(fern)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the fern is grateful, we turn to the rule linking gratitude to brilliance. The rule states that gratitude guarantees brilliance. Since the fern satisfies the antecedent, we can conclude that the fern is brilliant. This result moves us closer to establishing supportiveness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Using the newly obtained gratitude and the rule that gratitude leads to brilliance, we conclude the fern is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(fern)</logic>
</step>

With the fern established as brilliant, we consult the rule that brilliant things are supportive. The antecedent of this rule is satisfied, so we infer that the fern is supportive. At this point we have both a positive supportiveness claim and a rule that connects scholarly status to a negative supportiveness claim.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">

We have the fern being supportive, and we also have a rule saying that if something is scholarly then it is not supportive. Using modus tollens, the presence of supportiveness forces the negation of the scholarly condition. Therefore, we can state that the fern is not scholarly. This negation will be useful when we consider the disjunction involving scholarly status.

The disjunction tells us that the fern is either scholarly or responsible. Since we have just derived that the fern cannot be scholarly, the only remaining option in the disjunction is responsibility. By disjunctive syllogism we thus infer that the fern is responsible. This new fact activates the rule about responsibility and activity.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">

<logic>responsible(fern)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the fern is responsible, we apply the rule that responsibility implies activity. The antecedent holds, so we deduce that the fern is active. This directly contradicts the query’s claim that the fern is not active.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Given the fern’s responsibility and the rule that responsibility guarantees activity, we derive that the fern is active.</text>
<logic>active(fern)</logic>
</step>

We derived Fern is active, so the conclusion Fern is not active is False.
</think>
Answer: False

The third premise states that astuteness guarantees perceptiveness. This final conditional will allow us to conclude Jill's perceptiveness once we have derived her astuteness.

The fact that Jill is knowledgeable is given directly in the problem statement. This concrete piece of information is the starting point for applying the first rule about knowledgeable individuals.

examples.context: In the ballet company, the principal dancer is graceful. The choreography dictates that any dancer who moves fluidly is also aspiring. Furthermore, any dancer who is aspiring is considered precise. Either the principal dancer is not graceful, or she moves fluidly. The dancer is graceful.
examples.question: Is the principal dancer precise?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the principal dancer possesses the quality of precision. To answer, we will use the facts about her gracefulness, the conditional relationships between fluidity, aspiration, and precision, and the provided disjunction.

The rule linking fluid movement to aspiration is essential because it creates a bridge from the observable property of fluidity to the higher-level trait of being aspiring. Recognizing this connection will allow us to propagate information forward once fluidity is established.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the principal dancer moves fluidly, then she is aspiring.</text>
<logic>aspiring(principal_dancer) :- fluid(principal_dancer)</logic>
</step>

This second rule connects aspiration to precision, completing the chain needed to reach the goal. If we can show the dancer is aspiring, this rule immediately gives us precision.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the principal dancer is aspiring, then she is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(principal_dancer) :- aspiring(principal_dancer)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities: either the dancer lacks grace or she moves fluidly. Since we already have a fact about her grace, this statement will let us eliminate one option and infer the other.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the principal dancer is not graceful, or she moves fluidly.</text>
<logic>~graceful(principal_dancer) v fluid(principal_dancer)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that the principal dancer is graceful provides the concrete starting point for our reasoning. It will interact with the disjunction to determine which alternative must hold.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The principal dancer is graceful.</text>
<logic>graceful(principal_dancer)</logic>
</step>

We combine the fact of gracefulness with the disjunction. Because the dancer is graceful, the clause "not graceful" cannot be true, leaving the only viable alternative that she moves fluidly. This use of disjunctive syllogism gives us the fluidity property.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Because the principal dancer is graceful, the "not graceful" option in the disjunction is false, so she must move fluidly.</text>
<logic>fluid(principal_dancer)</logic>
</step>

Now that fluidity is established, we apply the first conditional rule. The rule states that fluidity guarantees aspiration, so from the dancer's fluid movement we deduce that she is aspiring. This is a straightforward modus ponens step.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since she moves fluidly and fluidity implies aspiring, the principal dancer is aspiring.</text>
<logic>aspiring(principal_dancer)</logic>
</step>

Having shown the dancer is aspiring, we invoke the second conditional rule. Since aspiration leads to precision, modus ponens again lets us conclude that the dancer is precise.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given that she is aspiring and aspiring implies precise, the principal dancer is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(principal_dancer)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Principal_dancer is precise, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Router Jill is secure. Either router Jill is not secure, or it is stable. If a router is stable, then it is fast. If a router is reliable, then it is not fast. These are the only pieces of information we have about router Jill and the network rules.
examples.question: Is router Jill not reliable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether router Jill is not reliable. The goal is to use the given facts and conditional statements about security, stability, speed, and reliability to reach a conclusion about reliability.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a router is stable, then it is fast.</text>
<logic>fast(router_jill) :- stable(router_jill)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a router is reliable, then it is not fast.</text>
<logic>~fast(router_jill) :- reliable(router_jill)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either router Jill is not secure, or it is stable.</text>
<logic>~secure(router_jill) v stable(router_jill)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Router Jill is secure.</text>
<logic>secure(router_jill)</logic>
</step>

We start with the fact that router Jill is secure and the disjunction that says either Jill is not secure or she is stable. Because we know she is secure, the first part of the disjunction cannot hold, leaving stability as the only viable option. This reasoning lets us deduce that router Jill must be stable.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Since router Jill is secure, the ‘not secure’ alternative in the disjunction is false, so the disjunction forces router Jill to be stable.</text>
<logic>stable(router_jill)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established router Jill's stability, we can apply the rule that stability implies fastness. The conditional statement guarantees that any stable router is fast, so we can directly infer that router Jill is fast.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Because router Jill is stable and stability guarantees fastness, we can conclude that router Jill is fast.</text>
<logic>fast(router_jill)</logic>
</step>

Having concluded that router Jill is fast, we look at the rule linking reliability to the opposite of fastness: a reliable router would be not fast. Since Jill is fast, the antecedent of that rule cannot be true; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Consequently, we infer that router Jill cannot be reliable, i.e., she is not reliable.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Router Jill is fast, and a reliable router would have to be not fast; therefore router Jill cannot be reliable, so it is not reliable.</text>
<logic>~reliable(router_jill)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Router_jill is not reliable, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the workshop, Drew and Sofia are both described as motivated carpenters. Sofia's motivation guarantees that she pays close attention to her work, so any motivated Sofia is also attentive. Moreover, whenever Sofia is attentive, she keeps her tools and schedule in order, meaning any attentive Sofia is organized. On the other hand, if Sofia follows a strict code of conduct, she would abandon the habit of keeping things organized; that is, any principled Sofia would be not organized. These statements capture all the given facts and conditional relationships.
examples.question: Is it true that Sofia is not principled?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Sofia lacks the quality of being principled. To answer this, we will use the facts about her motivation and the chain of conditional rules that link motivation, attentiveness, organization, and principled behavior.

The first rule tells us that motivation guarantees attentiveness for Sofia. This connection is crucial because it allows us to move from the known fact of her being motivated to a new property that will be used later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Sofia is motivated, then she is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(sofia) :- motivated(sofia)</logic>
</step>

The second rule establishes that any attentive person is also organized. Once we have Sofia's attentiveness, this rule will let us derive her organization status, which is needed for the final inference.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Sofia is attentive, then she is organized.</text>
<logic>organized(sofia) :- attentive(sofia)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a link in the opposite direction: if someone is principled, they cannot be organized. This contrapositive relationship will be essential for applying a form of Modus Tollens later on.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Sofia is principled, then she is not organized.</text>
<logic>~organized(sofia) :- principled(sofia)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Sofia is motivated. This factual premise serves as the starting point for the reasoning chain, enabling us to activate the first conditional rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Sofia is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(sofia)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Sofia is motivated together with the rule that motivation implies attentiveness, we can directly conclude that Sofia is attentive. This step follows the universal instantiation of the rule for the specific individual Sofia.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From Sofia being motivated and the rule that motivated implies attentive, we infer that Sofia is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(sofia)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Sofia is attentive, we apply the second rule, which says attentive individuals are organized. By Modus Ponens, Sofia's attentiveness leads us to infer that she is organized.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since Sofia is attentive and attentive leads to organized, we conclude Sofia is organized.</text>
<logic>organized(sofia)</logic>
</step>

We have established that Sofia is organized, but the third rule says a principled Sofia would be not organized. Since Sofia is indeed organized, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that she cannot be principled. This is an application of Modus Tollens, yielding the negation of the principled property.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given that Sofia is organized and that being principled would make her not organized, we infer that Sofia cannot be principled, i.e., she is not principled.</text>
<logic>~principled(sofia)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Sofia is not principled, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The disjunction states that at least one of two conditions holds: either the robot lacks the data_focused trait or it is steady. This broad statement will become decisive when we learn more about the robot's data_focused status.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the robot is not data_focused or it is steady.</text>
<logic>~data_focused(assembly_bot) v steady(assembly_bot)</logic>
</step>

We are given directly that the robot is data_focused. This concrete fact will interact with the disjunction to eliminate one of its alternatives.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The robot is data_focused.</text>
<logic>data_focused(assembly_bot)</logic>
</step>

Since the robot is confirmed to be data_focused, the option "not data_focused" in the disjunction cannot be true. Consequently, the only remaining way for the disjunction to hold is that the robot is steady, giving us the steady property.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>Because the robot is data_focused, the first alternative of the disjunction must be false, so the robot is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(assembly_bot)</logic>
</step>

Having derived that the robot is steady, we can now apply the first rule: any steady robot is precision_oriented. This yields the precision_oriented property for the robot, moving us closer to a conflict with the high_power rule.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since the robot is steady and any steady robot is precision_oriented, the robot is precision_oriented.</text>
<logic>precision_oriented(assembly_bot)</logic>
</step>

We now have precision_oriented, but the second rule says high_power would imply the opposite, not precision_oriented. Using modus tollens, the presence of precision_oriented forces us to reject the possibility that the robot is high_power, so we infer that it is not high_power.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>The robot is precision_oriented, but high_power would make it not precision_oriented; therefore the robot cannot be high_power.</text>
<logic>~high_power(assembly_bot)</logic>
</step>

We derived Assembly_bot is not high power, so the conclusion Assembly_bot is high power is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Master_steve is aware. Whenever someone is aware, they are respectful. Either master_steve is not fit or he is centered. Either master_steve is not respectful or he is fit.

examples.question: Is Mark trustworthy?

We are given information about Mark’s character traits and a logical relationship between them. Our goal is to determine whether Mark can be classified as trustworthy.

The rule that generosity follows from compassion is crucial because it allows us to move from a known fact about Mark’s compassion to a claim about his generosity. Recognizing this conditional link will let us derive a new fact later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Mark is compassionate then Mark is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(mark) :- compassionate(mark)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction presents two mutually exclusive possibilities concerning Mark’s generosity and trustworthiness. Knowing that at least one of these statements must hold will be essential when we later establish Mark’s generosity.

</step>

The fact that Mark is compassionate gives us a concrete starting point for the chain of inference. Since this property is directly supplied, we can apply the rule from Premise 1 without any additional assumptions.

<logic>compassionate(mark)</logic>
</step>

Because we have established that Mark is compassionate (Premise 3) and we know that compassion implies generosity (Premise 1), we can conclude that Mark must be generous. This follows the standard modus ponens pattern: from 'if P then Q' and 'P' we infer 'Q'.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Since Mark is compassionate and the rule links compassion to generosity, we infer that Mark is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(mark)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Mark’s generosity, we can turn to the disjunction from Premise 2, which states that either Mark is not generous or he is trustworthy. Since the first alternative is contradicted by our derived fact that Mark is generous, the only way for the disjunction to remain true is for the second alternative—Mark being trustworthy—to hold.

<text>Given that Mark is generous and that either he is not generous or trustworthy, we conclude that Mark must be trustworthy.</text>

Answer: True

<logic>wise(parker) :- impartial(parker)</logic>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Zion is respectful, then Zion is perceptive.</text>
<logic>~wise(parker) v skillful(parker)</logic>
</step>

The third rule tells us that impartiality guarantees wisdom for Parker. Since we have a fact that Parker is impartial, this rule is the bridge that will let us move from the known fact to the desired property of wisdom.

<text>The rule says that if Parker is impartial, then Parker is wise.</text>
<logic>impartial(parker)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>By Modus Ponens, since the antecedent holds, we derive Parker is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(parker)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>By Disjunctive Syllogism, one disjunct is eliminated, leaving Parker is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(parker)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Parker is skillful, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the ancient excavation site, the researcher Lane is known to be articulate and precise. Petra, another scholar, also possesses the qualities of being articulate and precise. Seth, a third investigator, is described as articulate. The scholars follow a set of logical traditions: whenever someone is diligent, they are courageous; whenever someone is reflective, they are diligent; if someone is precise, then they are impartial; if someone is articulate, then they are loyal; if someone is loyal, then they are precise; and if someone is impartial, then they are reflective. No other facts, disjunctions, or default rules are given.
examples.question: Is it true that Seth is not courageous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Seth lacks courage. The goal is to see if the available facts and traditions force Seth to be courageous. By tracing the chain of implications starting from Seth's known properties, we can determine his status regarding courage.

The first rule tells us that diligence guarantees courage. This will be crucial later when we manage to show that Seth is diligent, allowing us to derive his courage directly.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The tradition states that any diligent person is also courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(seth) :- diligent(seth)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links reflectiveness to diligence. If we can establish that Seth is reflective, we will automatically obtain his diligence, moving us closer to the conclusion about courage.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The tradition states that any reflective person is also diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(seth) :- reflective(seth)</logic>
</step>

The third rule connects precision with impartiality. Since precision is one of the facts we will eventually get for Seth, this rule will let us move from precision to impartiality, a stepping stone toward reflectiveness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The tradition states that any precise person is also impartial.</text>
<logic>impartial(seth) :- precise(seth)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that being articulate implies loyalty. Because the context explicitly states that Seth is articulate, this rule will enable us to infer his loyalty right away.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The tradition states that any articulate person is also loyal.</text>
<logic>loyal(seth) :- articulate(seth)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule states that loyalty leads to precision. Once we have Seth's loyalty, this rule will give us his precision, which is needed for the later chain toward impartiality and beyond.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The tradition states that any loyal person is also precise.</text>
<logic>precise(seth) :- loyal(seth)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule tells us that impartiality leads to reflectiveness. After we obtain impartiality from precision, this rule will allow us to conclude that Seth is reflective, setting up the final steps toward diligence and courage.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The tradition states that any impartial person is also reflective.</text>
<logic>reflective(seth) :- impartial(seth)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Seth is articulate is the starting point for the whole derivation. It activates the rule about loyalty and initiates the cascade of properties that follow.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>It is recorded that Seth is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(seth)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Seth is articulate together with the rule that any articulate individual is loyal, we can directly assert that Seth is loyal. This is the first new attribute we derive beyond the given facts.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>From Seth being articulate and the rule that articulate implies loyal, we infer that Seth is loyal.</text>
<logic>loyal(seth)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Seth's loyalty, we apply the rule that loyalty implies precision. Consequently, Seth is precise, giving us a property that will later be used to obtain impartiality.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">
<text>Because Seth is loyal and the rule that loyal implies precise, we conclude that Seth is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(seth)</logic>
</step>

With Seth's precision established, we invoke the rule linking precision to impartiality. This yields that Seth is impartial, moving us one step closer to reflectiveness.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Given Seth's precision and the rule that precise implies impartial, we deduce that Seth is impartial.</text>
<logic>impartial(seth)</logic>
</step>

Since Seth is now known to be impartial, the rule that impartiality leads to reflectiveness allows us to infer that Seth is reflective. This is a pivotal moment because reflectiveness will give us diligence.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,6">
<text>Since Seth is impartial and the rule that impartial implies reflective, we determine that Seth is reflective.</text>
<logic>reflective(seth)</logic>
</step>

From Seth's reflectiveness and the rule that reflective individuals are diligent, we derive that Seth is diligent. Having diligence means we can finally apply the first rule about courage.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>From Seth's reflectiveness and the rule that reflective implies diligent, we infer that Seth is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(seth)</logic>
</step>

Finally, using the fact that Seth is diligent together with the rule that diligence guarantees courage, we conclude that Seth is courageous. This directly contradicts the claim that he is not courageous.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Because Seth is diligent and the rule that diligent implies courageous, we establish that Seth is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(seth)</logic>
</step>

We derived Seth is courageous, so the conclusion Seth is not courageous is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the wildlife sanctuary, the orangutan is alert, the sea lion is alert, and the sea otter is alert. The orangutan and the sea lion are both energetic. Whenever a sea otter is alert, it is also nurturing. Whenever a sea otter is nurturing, it becomes playful. If a sea otter is energetic, then it is problem_solving. Finally, either the sea otter is not playful or it is energetic.
examples.question: Is the sea otter problem_solving?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the sea otter possesses the problem_solving quality. To answer, we must trace how the given facts and rules lead to that property.

This premise tells us that alertness in a sea otter guarantees it is nurturing. It will be useful because we already know the sea otter is alert, allowing us to derive nurturing later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any sea otter that is alert is also nurturing.</text>
<logic>nurturing(sea_otter) :- alert(sea_otter)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that any nurturing sea otter becomes playful. Once we obtain nurturing, this rule will let us move toward establishing playfulness, which is needed for the next inference step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any nurturing sea otter is also playful.</text>
<logic>playful(sea_otter) :- nurturing(sea_otter)</logic>
</step>

This rule links energetic animals to problem_solving. Our ultimate goal is to prove problem_solving, so if we can show the sea otter is energetic, this premise will give us the desired conclusion.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any energetic sea otter is also problem_solving.</text>
<logic>problem_solving(sea_otter) :- energetic(sea_otter)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides a logical alternative: the sea otter is either not playful or energetic. When we later establish that the sea otter is indeed playful, the first alternative becomes false, forcing the second to be true.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction stating that the sea otter is either not playful or energetic.</text>
<logic>~playful(sea_otter) v energetic(sea_otter)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the sea otter is alert is a starting point for several chains of reasoning. It directly activates the alert‑to‑nurturing rule, setting the chain in motion.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The fact that the sea otter is alert.</text>
<logic>alert(sea_otter)</logic>
</step>

Using universal instantiation, we apply the alert‑to‑nurturing rule to the specific sea otter we know is alert. This yields the conclusion that the sea otter is nurturing, bridging the gap between a fact and a new property.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From the sea otter being alert and the alert‑to‑nurturing rule, we infer the sea otter is nurturing.</text>
<logic>nurturing(sea_otter)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have the sea otter nurturing, we invoke the nurturing‑to‑playful rule. Modus ponens lets us infer that the sea otter is playful, moving us closer to a condition needed for the disjunctive step.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Because the sea otter is nurturing and nurturing leads to playfulness, we conclude the sea otter is playful.</text>
<logic>playful(sea_otter)</logic>
</step>

We have the sea otter being playful, which contradicts the "not playful" part of the disjunction. By disjunctive syllogism, the only remaining possibility is that the sea otter is energetic. This step crucially provides the energetic status required for the final rule.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Given the sea otter is playful and the disjunction "not playful or energetic", we can eliminate the first option and deduce the sea otter is energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(sea_otter)</logic>
</step>

With the sea otter now known to be energetic, we apply the energetic‑to‑problem_solving rule. Universal instantiation gives us that the sea otter is problem_solving, directly answering the query.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>With the sea otter energetic and the energetic‑to‑problem_solving rule, we infer the sea otter is problem_solving.</text>
<logic>problem_solving(sea_otter)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Sea_otter is problem solving, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The bird named robin works diligently. Whenever robin happens to be productive, that guarantees he is not hardworking. In any case, robin is either productive or resourceful (or possibly both).
examples.question: Is it true that robin is not resourceful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several pieces of information about robin’s habits and abilities, and we need to determine whether the statement that robin is not resourceful holds. The goal is to see if the premises force robin to be resourceful, which would make the negation false.

The rule tells us that productivity implies the opposite of hard work for robin. This conditional will be useful later when we compare it with the fact that robin is indeed hardworking.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If robin is productive, then robin is not hardworking.</text>
<logic>~hardworking(robin) :- productive(robin)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that robin must satisfy at least one of the two properties: being productive or being resourceful. This alternative will allow us to eliminate one option and deduce the other.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Robin is either productive or resourceful.</text>
<logic>productive(robin) v resourceful(robin)</logic>
</step>

We are directly told that robin is hardworking. This fact will clash with the consequence of the first rule if we were to assume robin is productive.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Robin is hardworking.</text>
<logic>hardworking(robin)</logic>
</step>

Because robin is hardworking, the conditional from Premise 1 tells us that if he were productive he would have to be not hardworking, which contradicts the fact. Therefore, the only consistent possibility is that robin is not productive. This step uses a straightforward contradiction (modus tollens) between the fact and the conditional.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Since robin is hardworking and being productive would make him not hardworking, we infer that robin cannot be productive.</text>
<logic>~productive(robin)</logic>
</step>

Now we know robin is not productive, and the disjunction from Premise 2 says he must be either productive or resourceful. Since the productive alternative is ruled out, the only remaining way to satisfy the disjunction is for robin to be resourceful. This is a classic case of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Given that robin is not productive and that he must be either productive or resourceful, we conclude that robin is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(robin)</logic>
</step>

We derived Robin is resourceful, so the conclusion Robin is not resourceful is False.
</think>
Answer: False

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The records state that any trustworthy person is also intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(ember) :- trustworthy(ember)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links persistence to trustworthiness. Once we can show Ember is persistent, we will be able to conclude that Ember is trustworthy, which then feeds into the first rule.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>They also record that every persistent individual is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(ember) :- persistent(ember)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a negative relationship: an industrious person cannot be empathetic. This will let us turn a positive statement about empathy into a denial of industriousness later on.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>According to the scrolls, an industrious person cannot be empathetic.</text>
<logic>~empathetic(ember) :- industrious(ember)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that if someone is witty, they cannot be principled. This is the crucial bridge that will let us move from a statement about principledness to a conclusion about the absence of wittiness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is written that a witty person cannot be principled.</text>
<logic>~principled(ember) :- witty(ember)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction offers two possibilities for Ember: either Ember is industrious or Ember is persistent. This alternative will help us decide which of these properties actually holds once we have more information.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Ember is industrious or Ember is persistent must be true.</text>
<logic>industrious(ember) v persistent(ember)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction gives another pair of alternatives: Ember is either not adaptable or Ember is empathetic. Because we already know Ember is adaptable, this forces the second option, letting us infer empathy.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Ember is not adaptable or Ember is empathetic must be true.</text>
<logic>~adaptable(ember) v empathetic(ember)</logic>
</step>

The third disjunction states that Ember is either not intuitive or Ember is principled. Later, after we establish intuition for Ember, this will let us deduce principledness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Ember is not intuitive or Ember is principled must be true.</text>
<logic>~intuitive(ember) v principled(ember)</logic>
</step>

The factual statement that Ember is adaptable serves as a starting point for applying the second disjunction, enabling us to derive empathy in the next step.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>It is a known fact that Ember is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(ember)</logic>
</step>

Since Ember is adaptable, the clause "Ember is not adaptable" cannot hold; therefore, the other side of the disjunction must be true, giving us Ember is empathetic. This use of disjunctive syllogism converts a negated alternative into a positive fact.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">
<text>From Ember being adaptable and the disjunction that Ember is not adaptable or Ember is empathetic, we infer that Ember is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(ember)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Ember is empathetic, we look at the rule that an industrious person cannot be empathetic. Because empathy is present, the antecedent (being industrious) must be false, so we conclude Ember is not industrious. This is an application of modus tollens.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Because Ember is empathetic and the rule that an industrious person cannot be empathetic, we conclude that Ember is not industrious.</text>
<logic>~industrious(ember)</logic>
</step>

With Ember not industrious and the earlier disjunction that Ember is industrious or persistent, the only way for the disjunction to stay true is for Ember to be persistent. Thus we infer persistence by eliminating the impossible alternative.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Given that Ember is not industrious and the disjunction that Ember is industrious or Ember is persistent, we deduce that Ember is persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(ember)</logic>
</step>

Having established Ember's persistence, we can invoke the rule that persistence implies trustworthiness. Therefore Ember is trustworthy, a straightforward use of modus ponens.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>Since Ember is persistent and every persistent person is trustworthy, Ember must be trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(ember)</logic>
</step>

Since Ember is trustworthy, the first rule tells us that trustworthiness leads to intuition. Consequently, Ember is intuitive, again by modus ponens.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>With Ember trustworthy and the rule that trustworthy implies intuitive, Ember is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(ember)</logic>
</step>

We now have Ember intuitive, and the third disjunction says either Ember is not intuitive or Ember is principled. Because the first option is false, the second must hold, so Ember is principled. This follows the same pattern as earlier disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,7">
<text>From Ember being intuitive and the disjunction that Ember is not intuitive or Ember is principled, we infer that Ember is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(ember)</logic>
</step>

Finally, we combine Ember's principled status with the rule that a witty person cannot be principled. The presence of principledness forces the negation of wittiness, giving us that Ember is not witty via modus tollens.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">
<text>Because Ember is principled and the rule that a witty person cannot be principled, we infer that Ember is not witty.</text>
<logic>~witty(ember)</logic>
</step>

We derived Ember is not witty, so the conclusion Ember is witty is False.
</think>
Answer: False

<logic>calm(skyler) :- content(skyler)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that fitness guarantees meticulousness for Skyler. Since we already have a fact that Skyler is fit, this rule will let us produce the property meticulous, which is needed for the disjunctive reasoning step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Skyler is fit, then Skyler is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(skyler) :- fit(skyler)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a logical choice: either Skyler lacks meticulousness or Skyler possesses content. This will become useful once we have established whether Skyler is meticulous, allowing us to eliminate one side of the alternative.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Skyler is not meticulous or Skyler is content.</text>
<logic>~meticulous(skyler) v content(skyler)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Skyler is fit is the starting point for applying the fit‑to‑meticulous rule. It ensures we have a concrete premise to trigger the derivation of meticulous(Skyler).
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Skyler is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(skyler)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule that fit implies meticulous to the fact that Skyler is fit yields meticulous(Skyler). This step is straightforward: the condition of the rule is satisfied, so the conclusion follows directly. Having meticulous(Skyler) now sets up the disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From Skyler being fit we infer that Skyler is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(skyler)</logic>
</step>

We now have meticulous(Skyler) together with the disjunction "not meticulous or content". Since the first option of the disjunction is contradicted by our new fact, the only remaining possibility is that Skyler must be content. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism: eliminating the false disjunct leaves the other as true.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Because Skyler is meticulous and the alternative says either not meticulous or content, we conclude that Skyler is content.</text>
<logic>content(skyler)</logic>
</step>

With content(Skyler) established, we can invoke the conditional rule that content leads to calm. Because the antecedent of that rule holds, we can confidently conclude calm(Skyler). This bridges the gap from the earlier derived properties to the final attribute we are testing.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Since Skyler is content, we can deduce that Skyler is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(skyler)</logic>
</step>

We derived Skyler is calm, so the conclusion Skyler is not calm is False.
</think>
Answer: False

<logic>sage(sylph) :- thoughtful(sylph)</logic>
</step>

The third rule connects being orderly with becoming thoughtful, creating a bridge between the first derived property and the second. It ensures the reasoning can progress stepwise.

</step>

The disjunction states that either the sylph is not a sage or the sylph is prophetic. Once we establish the sylph is a sage, this sentence forces the prophetic alternative to hold.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the sylph is not a sage or the sylph is prophetic.</text>
<logic>~sage(sylph) v prophetic(sylph)</logic>
</step>

We are directly told that the sylph possesses the battle-hardened attribute. This fact serves as the starting point for applying the conditional rules.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The sylph is battle-hardened.</text>
<logic>battle_hardened(sylph)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that the sylph is battle-hardened with the rule that battle-hardened implies orderly, we can confidently assert the sylph is orderly. This follows the standard modus ponens pattern.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From the sylph being battle-hardened and the rule that battle-hardened implies orderly, we infer that the sylph is orderly.</text>
<logic>orderly(sylph)</logic>

Having shown the sylph is orderly, we now use the rule that orderly leads to thoughtfulness. By applying modus ponens again, we derive that the sylph is thoughtful.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">

With the sylph established as thoughtful, the second rule allows us to conclude the sylph is a sage. This step continues the chain of modus ponens reasoning.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Given the sylph is thoughtful and the rule that thoughtful implies sage, we conclude the sylph is a sage.</text>
<logic>sage(sylph)</logic>
</step>

At this point we know the sylph is a sage. The disjunction tells us that either the sylph is not a sage or the sylph is prophetic. Since the first option is false, the only viable alternative is that the sylph is prophetic, which we therefore infer.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Since the sylph is a sage and we have that either the sylph is not a sage or prophetic, we must accept that the sylph is prophetic.</text>
<logic>prophetic(sylph)</logic>
</step>

We derived Sylph is prophetic, so the conclusion Sylph is not prophetic is False.

examples.context: Jack is ambitious. Whenever someone is ambitious, they are alert, so Jack's ambition makes him alert. Any alert person is also empathetic, which means Jack becomes empathetic because he is alert. Being empathetic guarantees cleverness, so Jack is clever. If someone is anxious, then they are not clever; this rule will matter later. Either Jack is anxious or Jack is logical – the situation is described as a disjunction. Whenever something is logical, it is focused, so if Jack turns out to be logical he will be focused. Finally, any focused individual is wise, so a focused Jack would be wise.
examples.question: Is Jack not wise?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the statement "Jack is not wise" holds. The goal is to see if we can derive a contradiction from the premises, which would make the negation of wisdom false. All the given facts and rules will be used to build a chain of inference leading to Jack's wisdom.

This rule connects ambition to alertness, which will let us turn the known fact of Jack's ambition into a new fact about his alertness. It is the first link in the forward‑chaining process.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is ambitious, then Jack is alert.</text>
<logic>alert(jack) :- ambitious(jack)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that empathy guarantees cleverness. Once we have empathy for Jack, we can immediately infer that he is clever, which will be useful later for eliminating anxiety.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is empathetic, then Jack is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(jack) :- empathetic(jack)</logic>
</step>

Here we have the bridge from alertness back to empathy. By establishing Jack's alertness, we will be able to claim that he is empathetic, setting up the next step toward cleverness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is alert, then Jack is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(jack) :- alert(jack)</logic>
</step>

This conditional says that logical status produces focus. Later, after we show Jack is logical, we will be able to obtain focus and then wisdom.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is logical, then Jack is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(jack) :- logical(jack)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule says that focus yields wisdom. It will allow us to reach the final desired property, wise, once we have focus.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is focused, then Jack is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(jack) :- focused(jack)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule gives a negative consequence: if Jack were anxious, he could not be clever. This will be used in a contrapositive style to rule out anxiety once we know Jack is clever.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is anxious, then Jack is not clever.</text>
<logic>~clever(jack) :- anxious(jack)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two possibilities for Jack: either he is anxious or he is logical. By eliminating one option, we can infer the other.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Jack is anxious or Jack is logical.</text>
<logic>anxious(jack) v logical(jack)</logic>
</step>

The only explicit fact we start with is that Jack is ambitious. This fact will trigger the first rule and start the chain of deductions.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Jack is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(jack)</logic>
</step>

Applying Modus Ponens, the ambition‑to‑alert rule together with the fact that Jack is ambitious gives us the new fact that Jack is alert. This is the first derived property we need.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From Jack being ambitious and the rule that ambition implies alertness, we infer that Jack is alert.</text>
<logic>alert(jack)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Jack is alert, we use the alert‑to‑empathy rule (again via Modus Ponens) to conclude that Jack is empathetic. This moves us closer to cleverness.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Because Jack is alert and alertness leads to empathy, Jack becomes empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(jack)</logic>
</step>

With empathy established, the empathy‑to‑cleverness rule lets us infer that Jack is clever. This fact will later help us eliminate the possibility that Jack is anxious.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Since Jack is empathetic and empathy yields cleverness, Jack is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(jack)</logic>
</step>

We have Jack being clever and the rule that anxiety would make him not clever. Using Modus Tollens, if anxiety implied not‑clever and Jack is indeed clever, then Jack cannot be anxious. Hence we derive ~anxious(jack).
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Given that Jack is clever, the rule that anxiety would make him not clever lets us conclude that Jack cannot be anxious.</text>
<logic>~anxious(jack)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction says Jack is either anxious or logical. Since we have just shown he is not anxious, the only remaining option is that Jack is logical. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>With the knowledge that Jack is not anxious and the disjunction that he is either anxious or logical, we deduce that Jack must be logical.</text>
<logic>logical(jack)</logic>
</step>

From Jack being logical, the logical‑to‑focused rule gives us that Jack is focused. This step follows directly by Modus Ponens.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,4">
<text>Jack's logical status triggers the rule that logical things are focused, so Jack is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(jack)</logic>
</step>

Finally, using the focused‑to‑wise rule, the fact that Jack is focused lets us conclude that Jack is wise. This completes the forward chain from the original fact to the property we care about.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>Because Jack is focused, the rule that focus leads to wisdom tells us that Jack is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(jack)</logic>
</step>

We derived Jack is wise, so the conclusion Jack is not wise is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Tess is known to be strong. Whenever Tess is strong, she is also experienced. If Tess happens to be friendly, then she can be counted on as trustworthy. On the other hand, if Tess is compassionate, she cannot be experienced. Moreover, either Tess is compassionate or she is friendly (or possibly both).
examples.question: Is it the case that Tess is not trustworthy?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the statement that Tess is not trustworthy holds. The problem supplies several facts and conditional rules that will let us reason about Tess's trustworthiness.

This rule links strength to experience; it will let us turn the known fact that Tess is strong into information about her experience. Knowing whether Tess is experienced is crucial for later steps.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Tess is strong, then Tess is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(tess) :- strong(tess)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that friendliness guarantees trustworthiness. If we can establish that Tess is friendly, we will immediately obtain the desired conclusion about trustworthiness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Tess is friendly, then Tess is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(tess) :- friendly(tess)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a way to rule out compassion: if Tess were compassionate, she could not be experienced. This will be useful once we have determined her experience status.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Tess is compassionate, then Tess is not experienced.</text>
<logic>~experienced(tess) :- compassionate(tess)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possible traits for Tess—compassionate or friendly. Together with other deductions, it will allow us to eliminate one alternative and pinpoint the other.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The statement says that either Tess is compassionate or Tess is friendly.</text>
<logic>compassionate(tess) v friendly(tess)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Tess is strong is our starting point. It activates the first rule, enabling us to infer something about her experience.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is a given fact that Tess is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(tess)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens, we combine the fact that Tess is strong with the rule that strength implies experience. Consequently, we can assert that Tess is experienced. This new fact will be used to test the conditional about compassion.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From Tess being strong and the first rule, we infer that Tess is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(tess)</logic>
</step>

We now have Tess experienced, but the third rule says that compassion would make her not experienced. Using modus tollens, the presence of experience forces the negation of compassion, so we conclude that Tess is not compassionate. This eliminates one side of the disjunction.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Because Tess is experienced and compassion would make her not experienced, we conclude that Tess cannot be compassionate.</text>
<logic>~compassionate(tess)</logic>
</step>

Given that Tess is not compassionate and the disjunction that she must be either compassionate or friendly, the only remaining possibility is that Tess is friendly. This follows by disjunctive syllogism, and it provides the antecedent needed for the trustworthiness rule.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Since Tess is not compassionate and either compassion or friendliness must hold, we deduce that Tess is friendly.</text>
<logic>friendly(tess)</logic>
</step>

With Tess established as friendly, we invoke the second rule: friendliness guarantees trustworthiness. Therefore, we can infer that Tess is trustworthy. This directly contradicts the query's claim.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Given that Tess is friendly and the second rule, we infer that Tess is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(tess)</logic>
</step>

We derived Tess is trustworthy, so the conclusion Tess is not trustworthy is False.
</think>
Answer: False

<logic>~vigorous(queen_bee) v seasoned(queen_bee)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the queen bee is precise gives us a concrete starting point. It provides the premise needed to trigger the rule that turns precision into neutrality.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The queen bee is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(queen_bee)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the queen bee is precise together with the rule that precision leads to neutrality, we apply modus ponens to derive that she is neutral. This step translates the concrete fact into a new property that will be used next.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From the queen bee being precise and the rule that precision implies neutrality, we conclude the queen bee is neutral.</text>
<logic>neutral(queen_bee)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the queen bee is neutral, we can invoke the rule that neutrality guarantees vigor. Again by modus ponens, we conclude that the queen bee is vigorous, satisfying the first part of the disjunction.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Given the queen bee is neutral and the rule that neutrality leads to vigor, we infer the queen bee is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(queen_bee)</logic>
</step>

With vigor established, the disjunction "not vigorous or seasoned" cannot be satisfied by the first alternative, because the queen bee is indeed vigorous. Therefore, by disjunctive syllogism, the only remaining way for the statement to hold is that the queen bee is seasoned.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since the queen bee is vigorous and we have the statement that either she is not vigorous or seasoned, we deduce she must be seasoned.</text>
<logic>seasoned(queen_bee)</logic>
</step>

We derived Queen_bee is seasoned, so the conclusion Queen_bee is not seasoned is False.
</think>

examples.context: Derek shows teamwork. Whenever someone shows teamwork, they are watchful. Whenever someone is watchful, they are sailing. If someone is steady, then they are exact. If someone is tactical, then they are not exact. Either Derek is not sailing or Derek is steady.
examples.question: Is Derek tactical?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether Derek can be described as tactical. The goal is to see if the premises force Derek to be non‑tactical, which would make the query false.

The first rule links watchfulness to sailing, so any time we can establish Derek is watchful, we will automatically get that he is sailing. This connection will be useful later when we combine it with other information.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if someone is watchful, then they are sailing.</text>
<logic>sailing(derek) :- watchful(derek)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects teamwork to watchfulness. Since we already know Derek shows teamwork, this rule gives us a path to derive his watchfulness, and consequently his sailing status.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if someone shows teamwork, then they are watchful.</text>
<logic>watchful(derek) :- teamwork(derek)</logic>
</step>

The third rule tells us that steadiness guarantees exactness. Once we manage to prove Derek is steady, we will be able to infer that he is exact, which will later clash with the tactical rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if someone is steady, then they are exact.</text>
<logic>exact(derek) :- steady(derek)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule states that being tactical forces a person to be not exact. This is a contrapositive style rule that will allow us to derive a negative about tactical once we have exactness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if someone is tactical, then they are not exact.</text>
<logic>~exact(derek) :- tactical(derek)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us an either‑or condition: either Derek is not sailing or he is steady. If we can show Derek is sailing, the first alternative becomes false, forcing the second alternative—steady—to be true.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction states that either Derek is not sailing or Derek is steady.</text>
<logic>~sailing(derek) v steady(derek)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Derek shows teamwork is the starting point of the chain. It directly activates the rule linking teamwork to watchfulness.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Derek shows teamwork.</text>
<logic>teamwork(derek)</logic>
</step>

Applying the teamwork‑to‑watchfulness rule to the fact that Derek shows teamwork yields that Derek is watchful. This step is straightforward modus ponens: the antecedent is satisfied, so the consequent follows.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Derek's teamwork and the rule that teamwork implies watchfulness, we infer that Derek is watchful.</text>
<logic>watchful(derek)</logic>
</step>

With Derek now known to be watchful, we use the watchfulness‑to‑sailing rule. Again, modus ponens gives us that Derek is sailing, because the condition for sailing has been met.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Using Derek's watchfulness and the rule that watchfulness implies sailing, we infer that Derek is sailing.</text>
<logic>sailing(derek)</logic>
</step>

We have established Derek is sailing, and the disjunction tells us that either he is not sailing or he is steady. Since the first part of the disjunction is false, the only way the statement can hold is if Derek is steady. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">
<text>Since Derek is sailing and we have that either Derek is not sailing or Derek is steady, we can conclude that Derek is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(derek)</logic>
</step>

Having proved Derek is steady, the steadiness‑to‑exactness rule applies. By modus ponens, we conclude Derek is exact.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>From Derek's steadiness and the rule that steadiness implies exactness, we infer that Derek is exact.</text>
<logic>exact(derek)</logic>
</step>

Now we possess Derek's exactness, while the tactical rule says tactical would make someone not exact. Using modus tollens, the presence of exactness forces the negation of the antecedent, so Derek cannot be tactical.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Given that Derek is exact and that tactical implies not exact, we deduce that Derek cannot be tactical.</text>
<logic>~tactical(derek)</logic>
</step>

We derived Derek is not tactical, so the conclusion Derek is tactical is False.
</think>
Answer: False

<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From librarian_arden being customer_friendly and the rule that customer_friendly implies accountable, we infer that she is accountable.</text>
<logic>accountable(librarian_arden)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Arden's accountability, we use the first rule (accountable → detail_oriented) to infer that she is detail_oriented. This again is a straightforward application of modus ponens, extending the chain of reasoning.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since librarian_arden is now known to be accountable and accountability leads to being detail_oriented, we conclude she is detail_oriented.</text>
<logic>detail_oriented(librarian_arden)</logic>

Having derived that Arden is detail_oriented, we confront the third rule, which states that ethical individuals are not detail_oriented. Since Arden is detail_oriented, the only way to keep the rule true is to conclude that she is not ethical, i.e., ~ethical(librarian_arden). This uses modus tollens, flipping the implication.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">

examples.context: Cleo is knowledgeable. Whenever someone is knowledgeable, they are active. Being honest guarantees that one is alert. If someone is witty, then they are courageous. Any analytical person is honest. If someone is alert, they become optimistic. Being active leads to being witty. If someone is productive, then they are not courageous. Finally, Cleo is either productive or analytical.
examples.question: Is Cleo optimistic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Cleo enjoys an optimistic outlook. The goal is to trace a chain of implications from the facts we know about her to the property of optimism.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that knowledge makes someone active: if someone is knowledgeable, then they are active.</text>
<logic>active(cleo) :- knowledgeable(cleo)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that honesty leads to alertness: if someone is honest, then they are alert.</text>
<logic>alert(cleo) :- honest(cleo)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that wit creates courage: if someone is witty, then they are courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(cleo) :- witty(cleo)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that being analytical implies honesty: if someone is analytical, then they are honest.</text>
<logic>honest(cleo) :- analytical(cleo)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule that alertness brings optimism: if someone is alert, then they are optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(cleo) :- alert(cleo)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule that activity produces wit: if someone is active, then they are witty.</text>
<logic>witty(cleo) :- active(cleo)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The rule that productivity prevents courage: if someone is productive, then they are not courageous.</text>
<logic>~courageous(cleo) :- productive(cleo)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction stating that Cleo is either productive or analytical.</text>
<logic>productive(cleo) v analytical(cleo)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Cleo is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(cleo)</logic>
</step>

We start with the explicit fact that Cleo is knowledgeable. The first rule tells us that any knowledgeable person is active, so we can immediately conclude that Cleo is active. This gives us a foothold for the later steps that require activity.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>From Cleo being knowledgeable and the rule that knowledge implies activity, we infer that Cleo is active.</text>
<logic>active(cleo)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Cleo's activity, we turn to the rule linking activity to wit. Since the rule says every active individual is witty, Cleo's activity lets us infer that she is witty. This new property will be useful for obtaining courage.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,6">
<text>Because Cleo is now known to be active and activity implies wit, we conclude that Cleo is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(cleo)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that Cleo is witty, we apply the rule that wit implies courage. Because witty people are courageous, we can assert that Cleo is courageous. This fact will later help us eliminate the possibility that she is productive.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Having established that Cleo is witty and that wit leads to courage, we derive that Cleo is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(cleo)</logic>
</step>

The rule about productivity states that if someone is productive, then they are not courageous. We already know Cleo is courageous, which contradicts the conclusion of that rule if she were productive. By contrapositive reasoning (modus tollens), we therefore deduce that Cleo cannot be productive.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Since courageous individuals cannot be productive (productivity would make them not courageous), we infer that Cleo is not productive.</text>
<logic>~productive(cleo)</logic>
</step>

We are given a disjunction that Cleo is either productive or analytical. Since we have just shown she is not productive, the only way the disjunction can hold is if Cleo is analytical. Thus, we infer her analyticity.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">
<text>Given that Cleo is not productive and that she must be either productive or analytical, the only remaining possibility is that she is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(cleo)</logic>
</step>

Analytical status triggers another rule: every analytical person is honest. With Cleo now known to be analytical, we can directly conclude that she is honest. Honesty will be the bridge to alertness.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">
<text>From Cleo being analytical and the rule that analytical people are honest, we obtain that Cleo is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(cleo)</logic>
</step>

The rule connecting honesty to alertness says that honest individuals are alert. Because we have just derived Cleo's honesty, we can now state that Cleo is alert. Alertness is the final prerequisite for optimism.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,2">
<text>Because Cleo is honest and honesty guarantees alertness, we deduce that Cleo is alert.</text>
<logic>alert(cleo)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the optimism rule declares that any alert person is optimistic. Since Cleo is alert, we can confidently assert that she is optimistic. This completes the chain of reasoning required to answer the query.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,5">
<text>With Cleo alert and the rule that alertness yields optimism, we finally infer that Cleo is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(cleo)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Cleo is optimistic, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In this ancient community, Anna, Grant, Harper, and Hayden are all described as relaxed. If a person is active, then they are responsible. Finally, if a person is vigorous, they cannot be responsible. No other facts or rules are given.
examples.question: Is Harper vigorous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the statement that Harper is vigorous holds true. The goal is to see if the available facts and rules force Harper to be vigorous or to prevent it.

The rule linking principled to active will become useful later once we have established Harper's principled status. It tells us that principledness is sufficient for activity.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Harper is principled, then Harper is active.</text>
<logic>active(harper) :- principled(harper)</logic>
</step>

This conditional connects the known fact that Harper is relaxed to a new property, creative. It is essential because it gives us a way to start the chain of derivations.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Harper is relaxed, then Harper is creative.</text>
<logic>creative(harper) :- relaxed(harper)</logic>
</step>

By stating that creativity guarantees principledness, this rule allows us to move from a creative state to a principled one. It will be applied after we obtain creativity for Harper.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Harper is creative, then Harper is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(harper) :- creative(harper)</logic>
</step>

The rule that activity leads to responsibility will let us translate an active status into a responsible one. This step is needed to eventually confront the vigorous rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Harper is active, then Harper is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(harper) :- active(harper)</logic>
</step>

The negative rule says that vigor would make Harper not responsible. This creates a tension with any derived responsibility, enabling a modus tollens argument later.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Harper is vigorous, then Harper is not responsible.</text>
<logic>~responsible(harper) :- vigorous(harper)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that Harper is relaxed serves as the starting point for the inference chain. It gives us a concrete premise to apply the relaxed‑to‑creative rule.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Harper is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(harper)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Harper is relaxed together with the relaxed‑to‑creative rule, we can directly infer that Harper is creative. This is a straightforward application of the conditional.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Harper being relaxed and the rule that relaxed implies creative, we infer that Harper is creative.</text>
<logic>creative(harper)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Harper is creative, the creative‑to‑principled rule lets us conclude that Harper is principled. The inference follows by modus ponens from the previous step.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Since Harper is creative and creative implies principled, we conclude Harper is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(harper)</logic>
</step>

Having established Harper's principled nature, the principled‑to‑active rule yields that Harper is active. This step continues the chain toward responsibility.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Given Harper is principled and principled implies active, we deduce Harper is active.</text>
<logic>active(harper)</logic>
</step>

From Harper's activity we invoke the active‑to‑responsible rule, obtaining that Harper is responsible. At this point we have a property that will clash with the vigorous rule.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Because Harper is active and active leads to responsibility, Harper is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(harper)</logic>
</step>

Since we have derived that Harper is responsible, the rule stating that vigor would make him not responsible cannot be satisfied if he were vigorous. By modus tollens, we infer that Harper is not vigorous.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Harper is responsible, but if he were vigorous he would not be responsible; therefore Harper cannot be vigorous.</text>
<logic>~vigorous(harper)</logic>
</step>

We derived Harper is not vigorous, so the conclusion Harper is vigorous is False.
</think>
Answer: False
